# Preliminary Curatorial Report Concerning Object A.1927.41  
## Provisionally titled: *Astrolabium Fabularum*  
### Department of Oriental and Comparative Antiquities  
Royal Museum of History and Natural Philosophy, London  
17 October 1927

Prepared by: **Dr. Elias W. Vale**, Keeper of Instruments and Manuscripts

---

## I. Summary of Acquisition

On 3 September of the present year, the Museum received by private conveyance a brass instrument of uncertain origin, accompanied by a packet of correspondence, a damaged field notebook, and a notarized statement from one Mr. Henri Delorme of Marseille.

The object was offered as an “early astronomical calculatory device, probably Levantine, possessing unusual prophetic associations.” The asking price, conveyed through our agent Sir Ambrose Tilling, was £4,800. After inspection, and over the objection of the Acquisitions Committee’s legal adviser, the instrument was obtained for £3,250, conditional upon the Museum’s agreement not to disclose the name of the immediate vendor for a period of twenty years.

Its material value cannot justify the purchase. Its scholarly value, should even a portion of the enclosed claims be substantiated, cannot presently be estimated.

I must state at the outset that I do not accept the language of “prophecy” used by Mr. Delorme and his associates. Nor do I regard the object as evidence that future events may be mechanically observed. Such formulations are too imprecise to be useful and too readily encourage the sort of intellectual disorder that has attended this acquisition from the beginning.

Nevertheless, the object has produced certain results in controlled trials which demand a confidential record.

---

## II. Catalog Description

**Accession number:** A.1927.41  
**Provisional name:** *Astrolabium Fabularum* (“Astrolabe of Tales” or “of Fables”)  
**Object class:** Composite astronomical instrument; unknown subtype  
**Material:** Brass, bronze, silver inlay, traces of electrum, horn or blackened ivory pointer-tip, mineral pigment in engraved recesses  
**Date:** Undetermined. Proposed ranges vary from 3rd century B.C. to 14th century A.D.  
**Place of manufacture:** Undetermined. Claims include Alexandria, Harran, Tabriz, Samarkand, and an unnamed workshop near Merv.

### Measurements

| Component | Measurement |
|---|---:|
| Overall diameter | 18.43 cm |
| Maximum thickness at central boss | 2.81 cm |
| Weight | 1.746 kg |
| Diameter of primary rete | 15.12 cm |
| Diameter of inner disc | 11.07 cm |
| Number of movable rings | 7 |
| Number of fixed engraved plates | 4 |
| Number of visible star-pointers | 37 |
| Number of concealed apertures discovered | 9 |
| Central suspension ring internal diameter | 1.92 cm |

The instrument resembles an astrolabe only in the superficial sense that it contains a mater, a rotating rete, engraved plates, and degree markings. It cannot be used to determine conventional latitude. The limb bears no recognisable scale of hours, azimuths, zodiacal degrees, or planetary houses. The outermost ring is divided into 360 unequal intervals, each marked by a minute symbol resembling, but not corresponding exactly to, Greek, Syriac, Coptic, Arabic, Pahlavi, and certain characters unknown to this department.

At the instrument’s centre is a pivot capped with a silver dome. Around it turn three principal components:

1. a pierced disc bearing animals, ships, towers, serpents, kings, and figures apparently engaged in ritual or catastrophe;
2. a seven-armed rete whose points terminate not in conventional stars but in small engraved scenes;
3. a dark inner plate, almost black beneath its lacquered oxidation, on which fine lines appear only under oblique light.

The reverse contains a shallow circular recess, closed originally by a plate of bronze held with six minute screws. When this plate was removed by Mr. Harcourt, our metals conservator, we found a cavity containing a layer of compacted blue-black dust, a curl of animal hair, and a folded strip of gold leaf engraved on both sides.

The gold leaf is now catalogued separately as A.1927.41b.

---

## III. Condition

The object is in unusually sound condition for an alleged antiquity. Brass surfaces show wear consistent with prolonged handling, but the movable elements retain a precision that would be remarkable even in a modern scientific instrument. No major component has warped. The central pivot is slightly eccentric by 0.18 mm, whether through manufacture or later distortion cannot yet be determined.

The instrument has a persistent odour when warmed by handling: not metallic, but resembling wet stone after electrical discharge.

This observation is entered only because it has been made independently by myself, Mr. Harcourt, Miss Elinor Firth of the Manuscript Room, and Constable J. M. Pell, who was stationed outside the examination laboratory during the incident of 29 September.

---

## IV. Provenance as Claimed

The accompanying statement gives the following chain:

- discovered in 1919 by a party of Armenian refugees sheltering in a ruined monastery near Lake Van;
- purchased from them in 1920 by Captain Lucien Barrot, formerly of the French Eastern Expeditionary Corps;
- conveyed by Barrot to Aleppo;
- obtained in 1923 by the antiquarian Yusef al-Haddad;
- acquired by Delorme in 1925 following al-Haddad’s death during the “Ash Wednesday disturbances”;
- retained in Marseille until its sale to this Museum.

This account is contradicted in nearly every material detail by the papers supplied.

The damaged field notebook, apparently Barrot’s, contains sketches of the object dated **11 May 1917**, two years before the alleged discovery at Lake Van. A loose page in the same hand refers to “the brass wheel taken from the vault beneath the House of Orpheus, west of Mosul.” No such site is known to the archaeological record. Another entry mentions payment to “the three sons of Iskandar, who will not say where their father found it.” The date on this page is illegible, but may read 1914.

Mr. Delorme’s notarized statement insists that Barrot never travelled east of Erzurum. Yet Barrot’s military records, obtained by the Museum through unofficial channels, place him in northern Mesopotamia in late 1916.

The possibility that the object was excavated or removed from a site without authorization is substantial. The Museum’s legal position is therefore uncertain. I have advised the Director that no public display, publication, or formal accession announcement should be made until the question is resolved.

A further complication is that Barrot is recorded as having died at Verdun in June 1916.[1]

---

### [1] Editor’s Note, 1954

Military records cited by Vale are not conclusive. There were two officers named Lucien Barrot attached to French services during the war, one killed in 1916 and another reported missing near Alexandretta in 1921. Vale seems not to have known of the second man. This does not, however, repair the chronology; the surviving Barrot’s service record has no entry for Lake Van, Mosul, or Aleppo.

---

## V. Inscriptions

There are inscriptions on five distinct surfaces.

### A. Outer Limb

The text consists of 360 short units, each three to six characters long. Professor N. A. Darzi has identified recurrent grammatical structures consistent with late Parthian administrative formulae, though the vocabulary is plainly not Parthian.

Dr. Darzi’s provisional translation of one repeated unit:

> “What is sung is given a road.”

Another:

> “What is counted remains below.”

A third, occurring at intervals of approximately twenty-seven divisions:

> “Do not name the sun before it rises.”

These phrases ought not to be treated as instructions until their grammar has been independently verified.

### B. Inner Plate

Invisible under ordinary illumination, this inscription becomes legible when the plate is held at an angle of approximately 31 degrees to a single flame or electric lamp. It is written in a cursive hand combining Greek letter forms with Syriac ligatures.

Miss Firth’s translation:

> “This is not the wheel of heaven.  
> This is the wheel by which heaven is persuaded to remember.  
>  
> Let no man ask it, ‘What shall happen?’  
> For it has no answer to what has no shape.  
>  
> Ask instead: ‘Which king will cross the ash-water?’  
> ‘What beast will eat the bell?’  
> ‘Where shall the blind bride carry the knife?’  
>  
> Then turn the small hand until the story closes.  
> What is enclosed in a story may be measured.”

### C. Gold Leaf, Obverse

The gold leaf is badly creased and has suffered corrosion at the folds. The language appears to be a dialect of Middle Persian, though three words are without parallel.

Tentative translation:

> “The maker is not the maker.  
> The first reader is not the first reader.  
> The tower was built after it fell.  
>  
> Keep the wheel wrapped in wool and silence.  
> Do not let it hear the plain speech of markets, courts, or battlefields.”

### D. Gold Leaf, Reverse

Written in a smaller hand, perhaps added later:

> “At the ninth turning it will begin to speak of the house in which it is kept.”

This line has become a matter of some concern.

### E. Central Boss

Engraved in letters too small to have been initially noticed:

> “For the dead stars, who require witnesses.”

Professor Darzi believes this is not a dedication but a technical designation: “dead stars” may refer to events considered fixed in a narrative but not yet occurring in ordinary time.

I record the interpretation without endorsing it.

---

## VI. Mechanical Peculiarities

The instrument does not respond reliably to position, hour, temperature, magnetic field, or atmospheric pressure. It has been tested in the Observatory cellar, under the dome, and in the sealed map room. No relation to stellar position has been found.

It does respond to manipulation of the small horn-tipped pointer on the inner disc. The pointer may be set against any of the outer-limb symbols. When turned clockwise through a complete sequence of twelve marked intervals, the rete shifts of its own accord by a small but perceptible amount. This shift does not appear to be caused by spring tension. Mr. Harcourt found no escapement, coiled wire, weighted mechanism, mercury channel, or hidden gear train.

The movement is accompanied by a low vibration, more easily felt through the fingertips than heard. Several witnesses have described it as resembling distant carriage wheels on frozen ground.

The disturbing feature is not that the instrument moves. It is that its final configuration has, on three documented occasions, corresponded to events subsequently observed.

The correspondence occurs only after the operator has articulated a proposed event in figurative, narrative, or mythical language. Direct factual questions produce no movement beyond ordinary manual rotation.

Thus:

- “Will there be rain tomorrow?” — no observed effect.
- “Will the Thames flood?” — no observed effect.
- “Will the river climb its stone banks and enter the houses of the sleeping?” — partial movement.
- “When will the black river take the rooms of the poor and leave fish in their beds?” — full movement, followed by a configuration later interpreted as corresponding to the flood of 14 September.

I recognise how this reads. I have preserved the original wording in the observation log precisely because any later account would otherwise invite correction, embellishment, or unconscious invention.

---

## VII. Controlled Tests

The following tests were undertaken under conditions intended, so far as possible, to prevent retrospective interpretation.

A sealed prediction protocol was used. Before operation, the proposed “mythic query” was written by one party, witnessed, sealed, and placed in the Director’s safe. A second party, ignorant of the written wording, operated the instrument after hearing the query read aloud. A third party drew the resulting configuration. Only after the relevant interval had elapsed were the seals opened and the records compared.

The defects in this procedure are obvious. They include the interpretability of images, the impossibility of defining the time range of an answer, and the risk that a sufficiently imaginative observer can find a pattern in any disaster. Still, some results have been disquieting.

### Test I: 8 September 1927

**Query, read by Miss Firth:**

> “Which iron bird will fall into the field of white crosses before the month has emptied?”

The instrument’s rete moved 17 degrees clockwise without manual force. One pointer, shaped like a bird or arrow, came to rest over a small engraved field of crosses. The inner disc revealed a line leading to the symbol for “three,” or possibly “third.”

**Subsequent event:** On 11 September, a Royal Aerial Survey aeroplane descended in fog near St. Bartholomew’s cemetery outside Croydon. Three occupants were injured; none died.

The correspondence is imperfect. The aeroplane did not “fall” in the violent sense, and the crosses were gravestones rather than the anticipated military field.

### Test II: 16 September 1927

**Query, composed by Dr. Vale:**

> “When shall the red king lose his crown in the house with no doors?”

No immediate movement.

The question was revised at Miss Firth’s suggestion:

> “When shall the red king be unmade beneath the roof that opens only for smoke?”

The instrument rotated abruptly through approximately 61 degrees. A crown-shaped pointer aligned with a square enclosure marked by four broken lines. The horn-tipped hand indicated the outer-limb sequence translated by Darzi as “seven,” “ashes,” and “witnesses.”

**Subsequent event:** On 23 September, the furnace roof at the Red King Ironworks in Waltham Abbey collapsed during an overnight fire. Seven men were present; all survived, though two were severely burned.

This result is the strongest recorded to date, and the most troubling, for neither Miss Firth nor I knew that an industrial concern of that name existed. It was identified only after a report appeared in the *Essex Chronicle*.

### Test III: 25 September 1927

**Query, proposed by Mr. Harcourt:**

> “What beast will eat the bell?”

This phrase was selected because it appears nearly verbatim in the inner inscription.

The instrument did not move for 43 seconds. It then rotated slowly until a canine or wolf-like figure lay beneath a pointer shaped like an open mouth. The bell symbol was positioned at the margin, intersected by three lines.

**Subsequent event:** On 28 September, a large stray dog entered the bell tower of St. Jude’s, Bishopsgate, during restoration works. It became trapped among the mechanism. At 6:12 a.m., the animal’s movement released the half-hour hammer repeatedly, cracking the principal bell. The dog was destroyed by a constable after biting a workman.

The relation between “eat” and “damage” is, again, metaphorical and unsatisfactory.

### Test IV: 29 September 1927

**Query, devised jointly by Vale and Firth:**

> “Where will the blind bride carry the knife?”

At 2:14 p.m., the instrument vibrated but did not visibly turn. At 2:19 p.m., the lights in the examination room failed. During the darkness, Miss Firth cried out and cut her left palm on the edge of a glass specimen tray.

When illumination was restored, the horn pointer had moved to the symbol of a veiled female figure. A narrow engraved line extended from the figure toward a rectangular plan resembling a room with two windows.

The query had been intended as a test of whether the device would produce a distant public event. Instead, the nearest available resemblance appears to have been selected: Miss Firth, who had removed her spectacles shortly before the blackout, carrying the instrument’s cleaning knife from the worktable to the cabinet.

No further tests involving Museum staff were authorized after this incident.

---

### [2] Editor’s Note, 1954

The original test sheets do not support Vale’s statement that no further tests were authorized. Two unsigned records dated 2 and 4 October exist in the departmental file, though both have been partially burned. The first contains the phrase “the drowned archivist.” The second is illegible except for the words “west stair” and “not again.”

---

## VIII. On the Apparent Requirement of Mythic Form

The central peculiarity of A.1927.41 is not merely that it produces configurations later interpretable as events. It is that the configurations are elicited only when questions are framed as stories.

This should not be misunderstood as evidence that the instrument “understands” metaphor. The term imports a mental faculty for which there is no basis. It may be more accurate to say that the device requires a certain class of description: one that identifies an event not by its ordinary particulars, but by its role in a pattern.

An aeroplane becomes an iron bird. A factory roof becomes a house opened by smoke. A trapped dog becomes a beast eating a bell.

Why this should be so is unknown. One possible explanation is that the instrument is fraudulent, and its operators are unconsciously converting vague imagery into later events. This theory is compatible with the known fallibility of human judgment but does not explain the mechanical movements witnessed under sealed conditions.

A second explanation is that the instrument contains a predictive mechanism based on information unavailable to us. This is not satisfactory. No mechanism can predict an event from a description that deliberately excludes its identifying particulars, unless the description itself possesses some causal relation to the event.

The remaining explanation is unacceptable in ordinary terms, but must be stated:

The object may not distinguish between an event and a story told about an event. It may operate upon events only after they have acquired the form of narrative necessity.

In other words, it may be incapable of mapping the future as such. It may map only those portions of the future which are already, in some fashion, myths.

I do not offer this as a conclusion.

---

## IX. The Ninth Turning

The phrase on the gold leaf—“At the ninth turning it will begin to speak of the house in which it is kept”—was initially interpreted by us as a warning regarding repeated operation.

We had, by the evening of 1 October, conducted eight complete activations in which the inner horn pointer passed through a full twelve-interval sequence and the rete made an autonomous adjustment. The count is uncertain because Test IV may not have completed a full cycle.

On 2 October, at 9:08 a.m., I entered the examination laboratory alone. The instrument had been locked overnight in Cabinet 7, beneath a wool covering as advised by the gold inscription.

The covering was folded on the floor.

The cabinet was still locked.

A.1927.41 lay face upward on the central table, though I had personally secured it in the cabinet at 6:40 p.m. the previous evening. I cannot explain this discrepancy. No sign of forced entry was found, and the key remained on my ring.

The instrument’s inner plate was visible without oblique light.

Its lines, formerly too fine and irregular to interpret, had formed a plan.

I recognized it as the ground floor of the Museum.

Not exactly, at first. The plan was circular where our building is rectangular; the east galleries were represented as a series of nested chambers; the central rotunda appeared as a seven-pointed enclosure. Yet the relationships were unmistakable: the Great Hall, the Egyptian Court, the staircase to the Natural Philosophy collection, the map room, the Director’s office, and—most particularly—the small examination laboratory in which I stood.

The laboratory was marked by a symbol I had previously taken for a tower struck by lightning.

A thin new line extended from that symbol to the west stair.

At the end of the line was the figure of a man carrying something round in his arms.

---

## X. Recommendation

I recommend the following measures:

1. That A.1927.41 be removed from the Department of Antiquities and placed under the joint custody of the Director, the Keeper of Manuscripts, and the Chief Conservator.
2. That all operation cease pending a review by persons outside this institution, preferably including a mathematician, a linguist, and a qualified stage magician or mechanic experienced in concealed apparatus.
3. That no member of staff describe future events in the instrument’s presence, whether seriously, humorously, or by quotation.
4. That the object not be stored in any room represented upon its own plate.
5. That the Museum’s west stair be closed until structural inspection is complete.
6. That Miss Firth be granted leave with pay, owing to the injury sustained during Test IV and her subsequent distress.
7. That this report be sealed.

I add, reluctantly, that the last measure may now be pointless. If the object’s apparent map is indeed a representation of this building, then the building may already be, in the instrument’s terms, a story.

And if it is a story, there may be no meaningful distinction between a map of its rooms and an account of what occurs in them.

**Signed,**  
*Elias W. Vale*  
Keeper of Instruments and Manuscripts

---

## Handwritten Addendum, undated  
*Found on the final page in dark brown ink; handwriting identified as Vale’s. The final lines are interrupted.*

I returned at midnight despite the Director’s order. The west stair has been chained, but the chain was warm.

The plate has changed again.

There are now figures in every gallery. Not symbols—figures. Small enough that one needs the lens, but distinct. Visitors, perhaps. Or attendants. They move when no one is looking directly at them.

The Great Hall is marked with water.

The Egyptian Court is marked with a black sun.

The laboratory is no longer marked by the struck tower. It is marked by a man at a table.

I thought that was myself, until I saw the second man standing behind him.

There is no second man in this room.

The pointer is moving toward the west stair.

I have understood the error. It was never predicting what would happen here. It has been arranging the Museum into a form in which the story can happen.

The figure at the stair is carrying the instrument.

I am carrying the instrument.

I do not remember taking it from the table.

dream_response:
**THE LIBRARY OF UNRETURNED THINGS**  
*Provisional Catalogue, East Annex, Shelf 9¾*  

---

### **HOLLAND, E. (attributed).**  
**_A Book for the Person Holding It._**  
No place, no publisher, no date. Bound in a material resembling blue cloth, though it has on one occasion been described as “the sleeve of a very patient coat.” 214 pages, except when fewer are required. Illustrated sparingly, but apparently to good effect.

**Call Number:** Δ/SELF/1  
**Status:** Available only when not requested.  
**Loan Period:** Until the reader understands it, or Tuesday.

#### Description

A volume whose text adapts to its reader’s private circumstances, linguistic habits, forgotten griefs, practical concerns, and—according to one disputed report—the contents of their pockets.

The title page consistently reads:

> **A BOOK FOR THE PERSON HOLDING IT**

The author’s name varies. Most readers report no author at all. A retired naval accountant found the name “E. Holland” printed in neat black type; he later admitted that E. Holland was the name of a schoolmate to whom he had owed sixpence in 1948.

The physical book resists ordinary bibliographic examination. Measured at 21 cm when shelved, it has been recorded at 19 cm in the hands of a child and 34 cm when inspected by Professor Merrow, who had brought calipers “to settle the matter.” The professor’s calipers were subsequently catalogued as a separate item. They now measure exactly 21 cm regardless of what is placed between them.

---

### Librarian’s Annotations

**[In brown ink, likely c. 1731]**  
Do not permit apprentices to read beyond the chapter entitled *What You Meant to Say at Supper*. Last winter, three junior attendants became incapable of ordering soup without weeping.

**[In pencil, c. 1894]**  
The book is not malicious. This distinction has been made repeatedly and does not appear to reassure anyone.

**[In blue fountain pen, c. 1957]**  
Returned by Mr. L. Petherick, who complained that it contained “nothing but instructions for fixing a bicycle lamp.” On inspection, it contained a moving account of the final years of the Roman Republic. Mr. Petherick did not own a bicycle.

**[Typed, unsigned, c. 1986]**  
DO NOT shelve beside memoirs. It becomes conversational.

**[Sticky note, undated]**  
It has begun correcting the catalogue cards. Kindly stop leaving blank ones within reach.

---

### Condition

Generally excellent. Spine uncracked. Margins contain annotations in the handwriting of readers who deny making them. A faint scent of rain, old apples, and whatever one had hoped to smell in a childhood cupboard.

Page 147 is always warm.

---

### Reader Reviews

#### **I. From the diary of Lady Agnes Vale, 14 October 1688**

> I was given a little blue book by the keeper, who looked at me with that solemnity men reserve for weather and women’s opinions. I opened it expecting divinity and found, instead, a practical account of my own household, written in a most impertinent but useful style.
>
> It explained where my missing silver spoon had gone, why my husband had become so fond of “late meetings,” and how to dismiss a cook without causing her to poison the jam.
>
> The final chapter was a sermon upon forgiveness. I confess it was very moving, though I suspect the book had been listening at doors.
>
> I should like another copy for my sister, provided hers does not mention me.

#### **II. Review published in _The Mechanical Reader_, April 1863**

> An intolerable fraud, and an insult to rational inquiry.
>
> The alleged volume presented itself to me as a technical manual concerning an engine I have spent twelve years designing. Its diagrams were excellent. Its calculations were elegant. Its criticisms, regrettably, were correct.
>
> On page 83 it advised me to abandon the project, reconcile with my brother, and seek employment in municipal drainage.
>
> This is not literature. It is coercion bound in cloth.
>
> I took it home in order to expose it under controlled conditions. By morning it had become a collection of blank pages, save for a single sentence:
>
> *“You have already built the machine you wanted. You are standing inside it.”*
>
> My wife has asked that I cease writing about the matter at meals.

#### **III. Comment submitted electronically by “mari_owl_92,” 7 September 2019**

> 3/5 stars because it knew too much but also because the ending was kind of obvious???
>
> It started out as this quiet little novel about a girl who works nights in a museum, which I loved, and then halfway through it turned into a guide to calling my dad after eleven years. That part was honestly rude.
>
> There were also recipes? One was for a cake my grandmother used to make, except the recipe had a note in the margin saying “you always added too much nutmeg.”
>
> I did. I never told anyone that.
>
> Anyway, I called Dad. He was weird about it, obviously. But not bad weird.
>
> The book disappeared from my bag before I could return it, so I guess no late fee? Please advise.

---

### Excerpt

The following passage was transcribed independently by six readers. Though each insisted it appeared on page 52, no two copies agree precisely. The text below represents the closest common version.

> You are looking for the door you believe you missed.
>
> There was no door.
>
> There was, however, a chair beside a window. You sat in it once, very briefly, while someone in another room washed dishes and pretended not to wait for you.
>
> If you wish, you may return there now.
>
> The chair will not remember you.
>
> The person will.
>
> Before proceeding, please remove your shoes from the bed.

---

### Handling Instructions

1. Do not read aloud to groups larger than two. The book becomes indecisive.
2. Do not attempt to photograph pages. Photographs develop into images of the reader’s hands, usually older than expected.
3. Readers who encounter a chapter titled **“The Entirely Avoidable Conversation”** should be offered tea and reminded that the library closes at dusk.
4. If the volume addresses a staff member by name, staff member is to remain calm and consult the deputy librarian.  
   - If the deputy librarian is named in the text, consult the kettle.
5. Under no circumstances should the book be placed in Returns without first confirming that it wishes to be returned.

---

### Acquisition Note

First listed in the library inventory of 1604 as “one blue book, rather personal.” No record of purchase, donation, or accession survives.

The entry’s earliest known marginal note reads:

> **It was already on the shelf when the library was built around it.**

This claim is architecturally improbable, though not conclusively so.

---

### Current Location

Shelf 9¾, East Annex, between:

- **_A Brief History of Apologies Never Delivered_**, and  
- **_The Complete Atlas of Places One Meant to Visit._**

If absent, check the reading rooms.  
If not in the reading rooms, check whether you are holding it.

---

### Final Note from the Acting Catalogue Clerk

This entry was revised on 12 March at 9:14 a.m. to correct a minor error in the description: the book does not change according to who reads it.

At 9:17 a.m., the correction had been crossed out in a hand resembling my own.

At 9:19 a.m., the catalogue entry contained the following sentence, which I do not recall typing:

> **The catalogue does not change according to who reads it. It changes according to who believes they are reading it.**

I have removed the sentence twice.

It is presently back.

dream_response:
## *Catalogue of Domestic Remains from the Submerged Settlement of Veyr*  
*Department of Littoral Archaeology, North Estuary Museum*  
Compiled by Dr. Ilyan Marr, Senior Curator  
*All translations are provisional. The more extravagant interpretations advanced by certain colleagues have been excluded wherever possible.*

The site called **Veyr** lies beneath forty-three metres of cold green water in the Basin of Ors. No civic archive, inscriptional monument, or human remains have been recovered. The following objects—almost all domestic—constitute the entire surviving documentary record of its inhabitants.

---

### 1. Household Key with Blue Thread  
**Accession No. VYR.07.114**  
Copper alloy, wool fibre, traces of salt and calcium deposit. Length: 6.2 cm.

A simple three-bit key, recovered from what was apparently a residential corridor. A faded blue thread has been tied around its bow, perhaps to distinguish it from keys belonging to neighbouring households.

The accompanying bone tag bears the inscription: *“For the door that opens when it is pressed from within.”* This is conventionally taken to mean that Veyran doors opened outward, an unremarkable safety feature in a dense urban environment.[1]

Curiously, no hinges were found on the interior side of any surviving doorframe. Dr. Sel Vanne has proposed that the phrase is metaphorical, referring to death. This is characteristically excessive.

[1] The same phrase occurs, with a different final character, on VYR.11.302. If read as *“the door that opens when it is pressed from beneath,”* the meaning is less clear.

---

### 2. Glazed Drinking Cup Marked with Daily Measures  
**Accession No. VYR.03.088**  
Ceramic, pale grey glaze; capacity: approximately 310 ml.

A common domestic cup. Around the interior are six horizontal lines, each labelled in a compact Veyran script. The lowest is translated as *first thirst*; the highest, probably, as *last air*.

The vessel was recovered with a small clay stopper lodged in its base. This has led some excavators to identify it as a dosing cup for medicine. I consider it more likely to have been used for water rationing, especially given the city’s apparently enclosed geography and the absence of natural springs.

The rim is worn only on one side, as though users drank while facing a fixed direction. Several similar cups possess handles on the inside rather than the exterior. The practical reason remains unknown.

---

### 3. Kitchen Drain Grate  
**Accession No. VYR.09.201**  
Bronze, perforated; diameter: 18 cm.

A circular drain cover from a modest cooking space. Its perforations form a stylised eye, surrounded by the repeated warning: *“Do not give food to the sleeping channels.”*

Silt beneath the grate contained fish scales, vegetable fibre, ash, and a substantial quantity of hair. It is therefore likely that Veyran drains served as refuse conduits, as in other early cities. The religious language should not be overemphasised; householders everywhere have endowed drains, wells, ovens, and similar necessities with superstitious significance.

The grate’s underside is polished to a brightness inconsistent with ordinary wastewater flow. More troublingly, the “eye” faces downward.

---

### 4. Child’s Rubber Ball  
**Accession No. VYR.12.019**  
Elastic resin; diameter: 9.4 cm. Recovered from the upper level of a collapsed dwelling.

This small ball bears a map of Veyr in red, black, and yellow pigment. Streets are represented as branching lines; districts are marked with domestic symbols: bowls, beds, hooks, lamps, and what may be a row of teeth.[2]

The map differs substantially from the excavated plan. Most notably, it depicts no perimeter wall, harbour, gate, or outer road. Instead, all routes converge upon a large oval marked *the warm square*. Children’s toys are not reliable cartographic sources, of course, and the image may represent an idealised city centre.

A second inscription, partly abraded, reads: *“When the Square turns over, hold tight to the red streets.”*

[2] The “teeth” may equally be roof tiles. Professor Dorr’s claim that the city was built “inside an animate structure” is not supported by the evidence and has received regrettable public attention.

---

### 5. Linen Towel with Repeated Household Mark  
**Accession No. VYR.05.776**  
Woven vegetable fibre, heavily mineralised. Preserved dimensions: 41 × 27 cm.

A coarse towel or drying cloth, found folded beneath a sleeping platform. Along one edge appears the common Veyran household mark: three descending curves enclosing a vertical line. It has been translated as *rain*, *breath*, *descent*, and, less plausibly, *rib*.

Chemical analysis found no trace of soap, oil, or ordinary laundering agents. The fabric contains instead an unusually high concentration of saline proteins, not all identifiable as human or animal in origin.

The towel is embroidered with the phrase: *“Dry the little ones before the bell.”* This may refer to children bathing before an evening curfew. There is no evidence that Veyr possessed bells, though the same instruction appears on a metal whistle (VYR.02.610).

---

### 6. Collapsible Umbrella  
**Accession No. VYR.01.042**  
Wooden shaft, bronze ribs, treated membrane canopy. Restored length: 71 cm.

Perhaps the most familiar object in this collection: a folding umbrella. It was found closed beside a doorway, its handle looped around a wall hook. The canopy is decorated with painted droplets falling upward toward a dark blue arc.

Unlike a conventional umbrella, the waterproofed surface faces inward when opened. The outer surface is intentionally porous.

This has been explained as a wind-break, ceremonial parasol, fishing device, or an example of incompetent manufacture. I favour the first possibility. The claim that it was designed to shield its user from water rising from below is plainly absurd, although the object’s damaged clasp bears the instruction: *“Open only after the ceiling begins to fall.”*

---

### 7. Domestic Clock with Thirteen Divisions  
**Accession No. VYR.06.330**  
Bronze, shell inlay, internal water-driven mechanism. Diameter: 14 cm.

A small tabletop clock, though its mechanism is unlike any ordinary timepiece. Its face has thirteen divisions rather than twelve. The final segment, marked with a closed eye, is disproportionately large.

The clock’s water reservoir is connected not to a gravity feed but to a flexible bladder of layered membrane. When compressed, it advances the hand by one division. The surviving calibration marks indicate that the device ran faster during periods of increased pressure.

An inscription on the base reads: *“Set by the long exhale, never by the lamps.”* This suggests a local timekeeping custom—perhaps tidal, perhaps meteorological. It is worth noting that Veyr lies far from any significant tidal inlet, a fact which has been repeatedly misrepresented by proponents of the “breathing city” theory.

---

### 8. Pair of Work Shoes  
**Accession No. VYR.08.051a–b**  
Leather, fibre cord, resin; adult size.

Sturdy work shoes with unusually soft soles. Each sole is patterned with dozens of shallow circular cups, presumably to improve traction on wet stone. The left shoe contains a pebble wedged beneath the heel; the right contains a fragment of translucent scale.

The interior lining is inscribed with a mundane ownership formula: *“Mera, of the fourth upper street.”* Yet the city map on the child’s ball identifies only *red streets*, *white streets*, and *deep streets*; no upper streets are shown.

Wear on the soles indicates prolonged walking on a gently curved surface. This may be attributable to poor paving, settlement of foundations, or the shoes having been used elsewhere before their owner brought them to Veyr.

---

### 9. Covered Cooking Pot  
**Accession No. VYR.04.167**  
Blackened ceramic with copper lid; capacity: 4.8 litres.

A family-sized cooking pot, remarkable chiefly for its tightly fitted lid. The lid is secured by four rotating clamps and bears the repeated instruction: *“Keep the steam in. The house must not hear.”*

Residue analysis identified lentils, small fish, root vegetables, and a lipid compound derived from some large marine animal. This does not necessarily indicate a coastal diet. Trade in preserved marine fats was widespread throughout the period.

The pot’s base is convex rather than flat, making it unstable on an ordinary hearth. Its exterior is heat-blackened on all sides, including the lid. A colleague has suggested that it was heated by immersion in hot water. I find this possible, if unnecessarily elaborate.

---

### 10. Lease Tablet for a Two-Room Dwelling  
**Accession No. VYR.11.302**  
Pressed clay tablet; 12 × 8 cm.

The most substantial surviving written text from Veyr. It records the tenancy of a two-room dwelling by one *Esh Varu*, together with obligations concerning lamp oil, refuse, “night water,” and participation in the quarterly sealing of the lower streets.

The relevant clause has traditionally been translated: *“The tenant shall not puncture the city’s skin.”* A more conservative rendering would be *“The tenant shall not damage the outer plaster.”*[3] The latter is preferred by this catalogue.

More difficult is the legal classification of residents. The tablet repeatedly calls them *“membranes”* rather than citizens, tenants, or householders. This is probably an idiom of mutual obligation.

[3] The same word appears on VYR.06.330, where it clearly refers to the flexible bladder inside the clock.

---

### 11. Emergency Whistle  
**Accession No. VYR.02.610**  
Bone or dense cartilage; length: 5.1 cm.

A small whistle, suspended from a cord made of braided hair. It produces two notes when blown: one high and piercing, the other too low to be readily heard but detectable by instrument.

The inscription reads: *“At the first inward rain: wake the sleepers. At the second: sing into the cistern. At the third: do not run.”*

This was likely an emergency alarm for flooding. The city’s final submergence may therefore have followed a catastrophic failure of its drainage infrastructure, though this explanation does not account for the instruction not to flee.

The whistle’s low note caused a cluster of small shell fragments recovered beside it to vibrate. The effect is probably incidental.

---

### 12. Polished Household Mirror  
**Accession No. VYR.10.904**  
Highly polished black metal, oval, 17 × 11 cm. Frame of pale enamel.

This object was found in a sleeping chamber, wrapped in the towel described under VYR.05.776. It is a mirror of exceptional quality. Around the frame is a domestic blessing: *“May the face below remain asleep until you have become old.”*

The mirror reflects normally in dry air. When its surface is moistened—even with a single drop—it shows, behind the observer’s reflected head, a pale curved form descending into darkness. The image is not present in photographs taken under identical conditions. Nor is it visible from every angle.

I have repeated the test only twice.

The enamel frame contains twelve tiny painted houses. In eleven, a narrow blue line lies below the windows. In the twelfth, the blue line crosses the roof.

No further handling is currently authorised.

dream_response:
## Excerpt from *The Xenotemporal Field Guide to Minor Habitats*, 9th Interstellar Library Edition  
### Habitat 44-B: The Intertonal Interval  
**Common designation:** The Quiet Between Notes  
**Accessibility classification:** Auditory-adjacent / temporally noncompliant  
**Human visitation status:** Strongly discouraged  

### General Description

The Intertonal Interval is an ecosystem occupying the measurable pause between two discrete musical tones. It should not be confused with silence. Silence is a broad absence or attenuation of organized sound; an interval-pause is a bounded event, defined by the cessation of one note and the anticipated arrival of another.

The habitat becomes accessible only when the following conditions are met:

1. A note must end cleanly enough to establish expectation.  
2. A subsequent note must be intended, though not necessarily performed.  
3. At least one listening mind must remain uncertain of the next note’s character.  

Under these conditions, the pause develops dimensional depth. To ordinary instruments it may last from 0.03 to 4.7 seconds. Internally, it contains valleys, weather systems, migratory routes, and geological formations estimated to be several million subjective years old.

The environment collapses when the next note sounds. This is not generally fatal to native organisms, which possess specialized mechanisms for surviving compression into auditory potential. However, foreign bodies lacking such mechanisms may be distributed across the succeeding chord as faint imperfections: a click in a recording, a tremor in a singer’s breath, an inexplicable sadness in an otherwise cheerful melody.

### Geography

The Intertonal Interval is arranged along an axis of expectation.

At its western boundary lies the **Decay Shelf**, a broad, glittering escarpment composed of the dying vibrations of the preceding note. Here, the air is warm, metallic, and increasingly thin. Harmonics fall from the Shelf in luminous strands, accumulating in lowlands as resonant fog.

Eastward spreads the **Held Plain**, whose ground is made of suspended anticipation. Footfall is possible but unreliable: an organism stepping too confidently may sink into a future that has not yet occurred. The Plain is crossed by long, slow rivers of unchosen melody. Their currents run toward every possible next note at once.

The far eastern region, called the **Threshold Forest**, marks the boundary nearest the arriving tone. Its trees are vertical silences, dark trunks branching into chords that have not been played. The forest grows denser as resolution approaches. At the moment the next note is sounded, the entire forest folds inward and becomes the note’s first instant.

Above all regions hangs the **Rest Sky**, usually colorless to human perception. Native observers describe it as “the blue of a question asked by someone who already knows they will not be answered.”

### Principal Species

#### 1. *Suspensia tremula* — The Trembling Suspension

The most common visible organism of the Held Plain, *S. tremula* resembles a translucent, six-limbed arthropod approximately the size of a comma. Its body oscillates continuously between two nearly identical forms. The creature feeds on unresolved harmonic tension, gathering it from the air with feathery antennae.

Trembling Suspensions are social. Large colonies arrange themselves into hovering shapes that resemble musical notation, though whether this resemblance is adaptive, coincidental, or communicative remains disputed. When threatened, a colony will collectively imitate a fermata: time around it slows, and predators often pass by without realizing they have waited.

Their average lifespan is estimated at twelve to twenty-seven pauses. Individuals may survive for years in performances characterized by slow, deliberate phrasing.

#### 2. *Cadentivora minor* — The Little Cadence-Eater

The Little Cadence-Eater is the principal predator of *S. tremula*. It is ribbon-bodied, blind, and covered in minute mouths that open only when a musical resolution becomes probable. Adults range from a few millimeters to several kilometers in subjective length, depending on the emotional intensity of the music surrounding the interval.

Rather than pursuing prey spatially, *C. minor* hunts by narrowing possibility. It emits a low, nearly inaudible pulse that encourages the next note to become obvious. As the number of plausible continuations decreases, Trembling Suspensions lose access to the uncertainty on which they feed. They become slower, brighter, and eventually still.

The Cadence-Eater then consumes them, leaving behind a brief sensation in nearby listeners: the feeling that a song has gone exactly where it had to go, and that something precious has been lost because of it.

Predation is most frequent in conventional tonal music. In atonal or improvised environments, where resolution remains unclear, *C. minor* populations are sparse and often malnourished.

#### 3. *Memoria auricula* — The Listening Moss

Listening Moss grows in sheets along the underside of the Decay Shelf and at the roots of Threshold Forest trees. It has no visible motion, no central nervous system, and no observed reproductive organs. Nonetheless, it records every sound that passes over it.

Each frond retains a microscopic fragment of the preceding note: not merely its frequency and amplitude, but the manner in which it was made. A violin’s bow pressure, a pianist’s hesitation, the faint mouth-noise before a singer begins—these are preserved within the Moss as chemical, temporal, and emotional structures.

When exposed to a suitable next note, the Moss releases stored fragments into the air. This produces what human listeners sometimes identify as “continuity,” “phrasing,” or “memory.”

Ancient colonies of *M. auricula* are thought to contain complete extinct songs. Researchers have attempted extraction. The results include several new symphonies, two contagious dreams, and the permanent disappearance of a research station from all recordings made after its disappearance.

### Ecological Relationships

The Intertonal Interval depends upon instability.

Trembling Suspensions consume unresolved expectation and are consumed, in turn, by Little Cadence-Eaters. The remains of both drift toward the Decay Shelf, where Listening Moss incorporates them into its archives. In this way, uncertainty becomes resolution; resolution becomes memory; memory nourishes the next uncertainty.

No organism within the habitat appears capable of existing in perfect silence or uninterrupted sound. All require the alternation between presence and absence. All are shaped by what has ended and what has not yet begun.

### Indigenous Folklore

Among the larger colonies of *S. tremula*, field translators have recorded a recurring oral pattern, transmitted through synchronized trembling:

> Before the First Note, there was only the Long Rest.  
> The Long Rest was lonely, so it broke itself into smaller rests.  
> In each small rest it placed a question.  
> In each question it placed a little creature.  
>  
> “Do not fear the next note,” said the Long Rest.  
> “It is only a door.”  
>  
> But the smallest creature asked,  
> “And what is on the other side?”  
>  
> The Long Rest answered,  
> “Another pause, if someone is listening.”

Scholars disagree on whether this constitutes a creation myth or a warning. The native distinction appears not to exist.

### Guidance for Human Visitors

Do not enter an interval while carrying an unresolved personal memory.

The habitat responds poorly to external expectation. Human visitors tend to bring excessive amounts of it: unfinished apologies, names not spoken aloud, melodies associated with the dead, questions that have survived beyond their useful lives. Such material attracts Cadence-Eaters in dangerous numbers.

More importantly, Listening Moss may preserve portions of the visitor’s internal continuity. Upon return, affected individuals have reported that certain memories no longer feel owned. They remain factually intact, but seem to belong to a person who ended one moment before the present one began.

Symptoms include:

- hearing faint applause after making ordinary decisions;  
- inability to tolerate delayed musical resolution;  
- recurring dreams of forests folding into a single piano key;  
- recognition of a song never previously heard;  
- grief for a life that appears, by all available evidence, never to have occurred.  

If accidental entry is suspected, do not immediately complete the musical phrase. Let the pause remain open, if possible. Sit without moving. Listen for the small trembling sounds beneath your own breath.

If you hear something answer, do not ask it whether it remembers you.

It does.

And somewhere beneath the next note, it has been waiting.

## From *The Nocturnal Biota and Other Oneiric Persistences*  
### Vol. II, Fascicle 7: The Perambulatory Architecture of Deferred Arrival  
**Compiled under the auspices of the Somnological Survey, Ninth Revised Dreaming**

---

### Order: *Ambulacra Inconclusa*  
### Family: *Vestibulidae*  
### Genus: *Latera* S. M. Vey, 1888  
### Species: *Latera domesticus* (“the hallway that continues after the house has ended”)

**Common names:** Infinite Hall, Receding Corridor, Domestic Elsewhere, the Last Hallway.  
**Deprecated names:** *Passageus interminabilis*, “Mother’s Upstairs,” and, in certain provincial dream registers, “the place behind the bathroom.”

#### Taxonomic position

| Rank | Designation |
|---|---|
| Kingdom | *Imaginalia* |
| Phylum | *Topologyta* |
| Class | *Interiora* |
| Order | *Ambulacra Inconclusa* |
| Family | *Vestibulidae* |
| Genus | *Latera* |
| Species | *L. domesticus* |

The classification of *L. domesticus* remains controversial. Vey placed it among the built environments, arguing that its chief characteristic is architectural extension. Dr. Hirako, however, maintained that it is properly a migratory organism which merely wears the appearance of a corridor, advancing before the dreamer at a rate calibrated precisely to prevent arrival.

The present editors favor Hirako’s position. A house, after all, does not ordinarily develop a new landing when observed from below.

---

### Diagnostic characteristics

*Latera domesticus* is most frequently encountered within a residence recognizable as the dreamer’s childhood home, though it may also parasitize hotels, schools, apartments formerly occupied, or houses visited only once in waking life. It typically manifests after the dreamer has been given an apparently simple instruction:

> “Your room is just down there.”  
> “The party is upstairs.”  
> “The bathroom is at the end of the hall.”  
> “They are waiting for you.”

The corridor then exhibits one or more of the following traits:

1. **Elastic depth.** Each door passed establishes at least one additional door beyond it.  
2. **Familial misregistration.** Wallpaper, carpets, framed photographs, and lamps may be recognizable individually, but never from the same remembered house.  
3. **Auditory concealment.** Music, conversation, or crying may be heard from a destination which cannot be reached.  
4. **Mild accusation.** The atmosphere suggests that the dreamer is late, irresponsible, or somehow already expected.  
5. **Terminal refusal.** At the apparent end, the corridor commonly opens into another corridor, a stairwell descending toward daylight, or a room containing only coats.

The species is not thought to be hostile. This qualification has caused no small amount of argument among survivors.

---

### On disputed terminology

The term **“infinite hallway”** is popular but technically unsound. No surveyed specimen has been demonstrated to be infinite; rather, the corridor persists until interrupted by awakening, false awakening, transfer to another dream habitat, or the onset of panic sufficient to destabilize local geometry.

The older designation **“receding corridor”** is more precise but implies visible motion. In most encounters, the hall does not seem to retreat. Instead, the dreamer becomes increasingly certain that they have already walked this distance before.

Professor Olasz proposed **“anticipatory architecture,”** defining the phenomenon as “a structure erected from the expectation of completion.” Her phrase is elegant, but has been criticized for obscuring the entity’s domestic habits. The hallway is not merely anticipated; it is almost always somebody’s hallway, and this somebody has often just stepped around the corner.

---

### Expedition notes

**Surveyor N. Pell, 4 March 1911**  
> Entered through a blue door in what I believed to be my grandmother’s kitchen. Hall measured approximately twelve paces in length. At the twelfth pace, observed the same blue door behind me, though I had not turned around. A child’s voice from below the floorboards said, “You missed it.” No child was recovered.

**Assistant Cartographer L. Adebayo, 17 October 1936**  
> Corridor wallpaper: pale green with repeating pears. Doors numbered 3, 3A, 3B, 3B, 3B, 4. Door 4 opened onto my own office, except every book on the shelf bore my name as author and none had been written. I declined to inspect their contents. Upon retreat, door 4 had become a linen closet containing warm towels.

**Dr. M. Hirako, undated fragment**  
> The important error is to think one is walking through it. The corridor is walking through the idea of you.

**Field Intern A. S., age unknown**  
> I found my parents at the end. They looked relieved. Then they asked why I had taken so long to be born.

---

### Figure 12. Minimal topological diagram of *L. domesticus*

*A small rectangle represents the dreamer’s starting room. From its right side extends a straight line labeled “hall.” Along the line are three short marks labeled “doors.” At the far end, the line bends back toward the rectangle and enters it from above, producing a loop. A note beneath reads: “Do not infer return.”*

---

### Behavior and ecology

The species appears most active during periods of unresolved obligation, impending travel, grief, adolescence, and the week before an examination. It is particularly common among individuals who have moved house repeatedly, though whether such movement attracts *L. domesticus* or is caused by prior exposure remains unsettled.

No reliable method of escape has been documented. Running increases the corridor’s ornamentation but not its rate of conclusion. Opening doors may produce temporary changes of habitat—ballrooms, abandoned classrooms, hospitals, dimly lit basements—but often returns the dreamer to a corridor of higher complexity.

A minority of subjects report success by sitting on the floor and refusing all destinations. These accounts are difficult to verify, as such subjects often awaken immediately thereafter and claim victory without furnishing adequate measurements.

---

### Related species

- ***Latera scholasticus*** — The school corridor after class has ended, distinguished by lockers that do not belong to the dreamer and an exam for which no subject has been taught.
- ***Latera hospitium*** — Hotel passageways with carpeting of impossible pattern; frequently associated with rooms numbered in fractions.
- ***Latera funerarius*** — A corridor leading toward a wake or family gathering, usually accompanied by the conviction that one is improperly dressed.
- ***Latera natalis*** — Rare and highly distressing; leads to a bedroom occupied by an infant the dreamer understands, without explanation, to be themselves.

---

### Field caution

Researchers are advised not to address an encountered hallway as though it were inanimate. In one notable case, a surveyor remarked, “This can’t go on forever,” whereupon every door in the passage opened simultaneously.

Nothing emerged. This is generally considered the worse outcome.

---

#### Footnote 61, appended in the hand of Dr. Elian Voss, final editor

It has become necessary to disclose a procedural concern. Over the last eleven years, I have found that certain descriptions submitted by independent dreamers correspond—not merely in structure, but in personal detail—to locations I have never reported: the narrow green hall of my first residence; the brass umbrella stand I inherited and discarded; the door at the end which I have always understood not to open.

Several witnesses have also described a man standing halfway down the corridor, holding a notebook, asking them what year they believe it is.

I had assumed this figure to be a projection of professional anxiety. I am less certain now.

If any future edition retains my name among its contributors, I request that the reader not mistake this for evidence that I have returned.

## Extract from *The Patient Reader’s Field Guide to Domestic Bibliofauna*  
**Third Revised Edition, with marginal corrections by the insects**

### Preliminary Note

A library is not a room containing books, though it may impersonate one for years. It is an ecosystem of dust, glue, paper, silence, appetite, and accumulated attention. Its inhabitants are commonly mistaken for objects because they have evolved, over several thousand years, an extraordinary talent for holding still.

Do not be deceived.

A book that has never been read is not dead, precisely. It is dormant, as a seed is dormant, as a sealed letter is dormant, as a bell is dormant between strikes. A book that has been read well may retain warmth for decades. Some, when opened, release a faint weather of their previous readers: tobacco, rain, orange peel, grief, train stations.

The following species are among the most frequently encountered in temperate libraries.

---

## I. *Novella domesticus* — The Hearth Novella

**Common names:** Pocket tale, railway book, little companion  
**Size:** 90–180 pages, usually warm-blooded in the hand  
**Habitat:** Bedside tables, coat pockets, holiday cottages, waiting rooms with poor lighting

The Hearth Novella is perhaps the most companionable of all book species. It is compact, self-contained, and easily startled by interruption. When carried in a pocket, it will often settle itself open at its preferred page, a behavior incorrectly attributed to weakened binding.

Its natural coloration ranges from faded cream to bright, hopeful red. Individuals are known to acquire scars: coffee rings, dog-eared corners, a pressed leaf at page 47. These marks are not injuries in the usual sense. They are territorial signals indicating that the book has been allowed into a human life.

### Diet

The Hearth Novella feeds chiefly on concentrated attention—especially the attention available during journeys, illnesses, and mild social alienation. It thrives on train delays. A commuter reading one during a long interruption may sustain it for years.

It is less tolerant of divided attention than larger species. Attempts to read a novella while watching television, answering messages, and ordering dinner commonly result in a thin, papery malaise.

### Reproduction

Reproduction occurs through recommendation. When a reader says, “It’s only a small one, but it stayed with me,” a viable spore is released. If the listener acquires another copy within a season, the species has successfully propagated.

In particularly favorable conditions, a novella may reproduce through lending. The lender hands it over saying, “You can have it back whenever,” thereby performing the ancient rite of hopeful separation.

### Predators

Its chief predator is the Overfull Tote Bag, within which it may be crushed beneath chargers, receipts, and a single unwrapped pear.

It is also vulnerable to the phrase “I’ll save it for a weekend when I can really appreciate it,” which has caused the extinction of countless wild reading opportunities.

### Relationship with readers

The Hearth Novella enters into a gentle mutualism with solitary readers. The reader offers warmth, time, and the occasional tear; the novella gives the reader the impression of having briefly lived elsewhere, usually somewhere more rain-soaked and morally complicated.

A well-read individual may remain in a household for generations, though it is often misplaced precisely when most needed.

---

## II. *Encyclopaedia grandis* — The Shelfback Colossus

**Common names:** Reference beast, domestic monument, the volume nobody is allowed to throw away  
**Size:** 600–2,000 pages; may exceed the lifting capacity of an average aunt  
**Habitat:** Lower shelves, inherited studies, institutions with brass lamps

The Shelfback Colossus is one of the longest-lived book species. Mature specimens possess dense, layered bodies and may be covered in a protective hide of cloth, leather, or a glossy coating resembling the skin of a very patient reptile.

They are not often read continuously. Indeed, a Colossus may go years without being opened, yet remain healthy so long as it is consulted occasionally and regarded with reverence.

The young are sleek and brightly jacketed. Older individuals become swollen at the spine and emit, when opened, the characteristic scent known to bibliologists as *tempus compressum*: a mixture of vanilla, attic, and low-level authority.

### Diet

The Colossus feeds on questions asked in sudden seriousness:

- “What exactly is a fjord?”
- “Was that before or after the war?”
- “How many moons does Jupiter have now?”
- “What is the proper collective noun for ferrets?”

It can survive for decades on a single well-asked question, particularly if followed by several minutes of solemn page-turning.

### Reproduction

Colossi reproduce by edition. When a younger, more updated specimen appears, the elder does not die immediately. Instead, it migrates to a back shelf and develops a formidable aura of historical dignity.

In captivity, old Colossi often reproduce indirectly by making their owners feel guilty. This guilt may cause the purchase of newer reference works, which then grow large and equally neglected.

### Predators

The principal predator is the Search Engine, a swift, luminous creature that answers questions in fragments and leaves no spoor. It has devastated Colossus populations in private homes.

Other threats include downsizing, minimalism, and adult children who ask, with the terrible practicality of their kind, “Do you actually use this?”

### Relationship with readers

The Shelfback Colossus offers readers a rare and valuable symbiosis: it permits them to feel knowledgeable while understanding only a paragraph at a time.

In return, readers provide a stable shelf, occasional dusting, and the firm belief that one day the Colossus will be needed in an emergency.

Sometimes it is.

---

## III. *Mysteria nocturna* — The Midnight Mystery

**Common names:** Whodunit, bedside hunter, page-turner  
**Size:** Variable; commonly found in sleek black, blue, or blood-red plumage  
**Habitat:** Nightstands, holiday luggage, baths, airports, suspiciously quiet houses

The Midnight Mystery is a crepuscular species. It appears placid in daylight, but after 10 p.m. becomes highly active, opening itself repeatedly in the reader’s hands and inducing a state known as “one more chapter.”

Field observers should be wary: this phrase is not descriptive but parasitic. Once spoken, it reproduces approximately every fourteen pages until dawn.

The Mystery’s internal structure is composed of trails, false trails, suspicious footprints, and at least one person who seems too nice.

### Diet

It feeds on uncertainty, particularly the small domestic uncertainties that accumulate after dark: an unexplained floorboard, a neighbor’s late car, the knowledge that one has forgotten to lock something.

It will also consume tea, biscuits, and sleep with astonishing efficiency.

### Reproduction

Mysteries reproduce through speculation. A reader who closes the book, points at a character, and announces, “It’s obviously the sister,” has already become part of the breeding cycle.

The species is especially fecund in book clubs, where competing theories create a thick reproductive fog. Under optimal conditions, twelve identical copies may hatch in a circle around a bowl of olives.

### Predators

Its natural predator is exhaustion. Many adult Mysteries are found face-down at 2:13 a.m., their readers unconscious beside them.

A more dangerous predator is premature revelation: a careless review, an overenthusiastic friend, or the back-cover blurb that says too much. Such attacks can leave a Mystery structurally intact but spiritually hollow.

### Relationship with readers

The Midnight Mystery provides alertness, suspicion, and temporary mastery over chaos. In exchange, readers surrender sleep and begin looking sideways at harmless spouses, vicars, gardeners, and antique dealers.

A healthy relationship ends with the reader saying, “I knew it,” despite having been entirely wrong.

---

## IV. *Poema fugax* — The Elusive Lyric

**Common names:** Little poem, margin-haunter, weather-maker  
**Size:** Often tiny; sometimes only fourteen lines, though capable of occupying a person for forty years  
**Habitat:** Upper shelves, coat pockets, old anthologies, the space between memory and language

The Elusive Lyric is easily overlooked because of its small size and stillness. It is commonly found nested among larger collections, where it may remain invisible until a reader is in precisely the right condition of heart.

Attempts to locate a particular lyric often fail. The researcher may search fifty pages, become irritated, and conclude that the poem was never very good. Then, weeks later, it will appear uninvited on a bus, at a funeral, while washing dishes, or during a moment of inexplicable happiness.

### Diet

The Lyric feeds on silence, repetition, and things not quite said. It is especially nourished by pauses after conversations, windows during rain, and the last light on laundry.

Unlike most book species, it can survive on very little attention. A single rereading every ten years may be sufficient.

### Reproduction

Its reproductive methods remain poorly understood. Some evidence suggests that a poem reproduces when a reader copies one line into a notebook without attribution. Other evidence indicates that it propagates through misquotation, which, though biologically successful, is frowned upon by taxonomists.

A Lyric may also lodge in the speech of a reader and emerge decades later, altered but alive.

### Predators

The principal predators are haste and embarrassment. The Lyric is particularly vulnerable to readers who say, “I don’t really get poetry,” as though understanding were the only available form of companionship.

It is also preyed upon by decorative quotation culture, in which it may be removed from its habitat, printed in elegant script on a cushion, and deprived of all its teeth.

### Relationship with readers

The Elusive Lyric asks almost nothing of its reader except patience. In exchange, it may provide a sentence for an otherwise wordless year.

There are readers who have been kept alive, quietly and without spectacle, by one small poem. The poem itself will never know. Or perhaps it knows and has the grace not to mention it.

---

## V. *Romanticus serialis* — The Longcourt Romance

**Common names:** Love story, historical seducer, duchess-book, serial heart  
**Size:** Frequently substantial; may occur in trilogies, quartets, dynasties, or alarming chains of twenty-seven  
**Habitat:** Beach bags, bathtubs, commuter trains, hidden behind “more respectable” books

The Longcourt Romance is among the most unfairly maligned inhabitants of the library. Recognizable by its lush cover plumage and tendency to feature windswept people with incomplete clothing, it is in fact a sophisticated social animal.

Many members of the species maintain elaborate habitats involving castles, bakeries, dragons, naval officers, rival families, magical academies, or villages in which the local florist is mysteriously unmarried.

### Diet

It feeds on longing: not merely romantic longing, but the broad mammalian desire to be seen clearly, chosen freely, and kissed at an appropriate narrative moment.

It also consumes injustice, misunderstandings, inherited estates, and emotionally unavailable aristocrats. A single brooding duke can nourish an entire breeding colony.

### Reproduction

The Longcourt Romance reproduces in series. At the conclusion of one volume, a minor character—often a sharp-tongued sibling or a grieving sea captain—will glance toward the horizon with unresolved feelings. This is the mating call.

Within eighteen months, that character will acquire a book of their own.

Some populations reproduce through “special editions,” which develop gilded edges and floral endpapers in order to attract collectors during the winter season.

### Predators

Its primary predator is shame, a human-borne fungus that causes readers to hide healthy specimens inside plain covers.

It is also threatened by the claim that its endings are “predictable.” This is a misunderstanding of its ecology. A Romance does not promise surprise; it promises return. No one criticizes spring for arriving again.

### Relationship with readers

The Longcourt Romance forms powerful bonds with readers recovering from grief, fatigue, heartbreak, bureaucracies, or the general weather of the world. The reader provides rapid page-turning and emotional weather; the book provides a place where devotion is articulate and dinner is usually served eventually.

Readers should not be embarrassed to be seen in the company of one. The species has survived worse critics than that.

---

## Conservation Concern: The Quieting of the Stacks

The gravest threat to all bibliofauna is not fire, flood, mildew, censorship, or even the notorious Silverfish (*Lepisma bibliovorax*), though each has done its share of damage.

It is the disappearance of unclaimed time.

Books do not require much. A shelf. Reasonable dryness. The occasional hand. But they cannot thrive where every minute has been divided, measured, sold, interrupted, optimized, and illuminated by notifications.

In many modern habitats, one finds books preserved beautifully but unread: bright-backed, alphabetized, dustless, faintly starving. They stand in rows like animals behind glass, waiting for the old reciprocal arrangement to resume. A human brings an hour of undistracted life. The book, in return, makes that hour larger than it was.

Where this exchange ceases, libraries remain—but become quieter in the wrong way.

Not the quiet of snow, or prayer, or a reader pausing over a sentence.

The quiet of a forest after the birds have gone.
