dream_response:
## The Returning Light of Nacre

### World: Nacre

Nacre is a rocky planet orbiting a small red star. It is tidally locked: one hemisphere faces the star continuously, while the opposite hemisphere lies beneath permanent darkness.

The dayward interior is too hot for human settlement. Its shallow seas evaporate into a near-constant ceiling of cloud, and its exposed rock is baked and fractured by heat. The nightward interior is a frozen desert where atmospheric gases slowly settle as snow in the deepest basins.

Between them lies a habitable belt roughly four hundred kilometres wide: the **Gloam**. Here the star hangs low above the horizon, copper-red through haze. Temperatures range from cool maritime weather near the dayward side to bitter frost near the nightward edge.

Air flows almost continuously from the hot hemisphere toward the cold one. The wind is strongest in gaps, canyons, and engineered channels. Every building in the Gloam has a dayward face built low and armored, and a nightward face insulated against freezing spray and wind-driven rime.

Nacre’s native ecology is simple but abundant. Dark, tough mats of photosynthetic lichen cling to warm rock. They use the dim red light and trap moisture from the constant air current. Pale “sail plants” grow on flexible stalks; their thin leaves turn edge-on to the wind and broadside to the star. In sheltered ravines, people cultivate them for oil, fibre, and food.

The settlements follow the Gloam in a long, broken ring. Most are connected by rail lines that run roughly parallel to the terminator, avoiding travel either dayward into heat or nightward into cold.

The economy rests on water, heat, and transit. Dayward condensers collect vapor from the hot air; nightward ice crews mine ancient glaciers; the settlements trade stored water, lichen oil, machine parts, and power from wind stations built in the strongest passes.

### The Strange Phenomenon: Returning Light

Nacre’s orbit is slightly eccentric. Though it keeps one face generally toward its star, its rotation is not perfectly uniform. Over the course of each thirty-two-day orbit, the star appears to drift eastward in the sky, slow to a stop, then move westward for a time before reversing again.

This is called **the return**.

At the two ends of the motion, the sun seems to hesitate for nearly a day. Shadows shorten, lengthen, and then reverse their apparent movement. A shadow angle that occurs at one point in the cycle will occur again several hours later, with the star in nearly the same position.

The climate does not reverse so quickly. The wind continues dayward-to-nightward, and the fog banks, frost lines, and thermal currents lag behind the returning light by many hours.

People on Nacre learn early not to tell time by shadows.

Most do.

---

# The Murder at Vesper Station

Vesper Station was a settlement of three thousand people built on a basalt ridge near the coldward edge of the Gloam. It had water ledgers, a rail depot, two greenhouses, a windworks complex, and one narrow service throat through which the station’s excess heat was vented nightward.

The dead woman was **Orla Fen**, Vesper’s water registrar.

Orla controlled the records that determined which farms, factories, and households received water allocations. She was not loved, but she was trusted. On the evening of the sixth day of the return, she was found dead beneath a fallen wind-baffle in Service Throat Three.

The baffle was a heavy sheet of insulated metal, normally held above the passage on a maintenance rail. It had come loose in the wind and struck her on the head.

At first, it seemed an accident.

Then Inspector Sima Darel noticed the frost.

---

## The Five Suspects

### 1. Edda Vale — Deputy Administrator

Edda managed Vesper’s civic accounts and answered to the regional water authority.

Orla had recently requested access to old allocation records that only Edda could authorize. If Orla had found evidence of diverted water credits, Edda’s career—and perhaps her freedom—would have ended.

Edda said she last saw Orla alive at 16:50.

### 2. Ilyan Sere — Greenhouse Director

Ilyan ran the station’s sail-plant conservatories. His greenhouses used more heat and water than their legal quota allowed.

Orla had informed him that she intended to cut his allocation at the next quarter’s review. A reduction would have killed his crops and put two hundred workers out of employment.

Ilyan claimed to have been in Greenhouse Two all afternoon.

### 3. Toma Rusk — Rail Captain

Toma oversaw freight trains carrying ice from the nightward mines.

Orla had been investigating discrepancies between the official ice manifests and the amount delivered to Vesper. Toma was widely suspected of selling unregistered ice to private growers.

He said he was on a maintenance run along the eastern rail from noon until dusk.

### 4. Dr. Nadi Quill — Ecologist

Nadi studied Nacre’s native sail plants and opposed the planned expansion of Vesper’s nightward industrial district.

Orla had supported the expansion because it promised more storage capacity and more jobs. Nadi believed the project would destroy a rare lichen basin.

She had argued with Orla publicly that morning.

### 5. Pera Sol — Windworks Foreman

Pera maintained the wind-baffles, pressure doors, and heat vents. The baffle that killed Orla had passed inspection only two days earlier.

If it had been neglected, Pera faced criminal liability. More quietly, Orla had learned that Pera had falsified several maintenance reports to conceal shortages of replacement parts.

Pera said he had inspected Service Throat Three at 15:40 and found it empty.

---

# Timeline

All times are Vesper Civil Time.

| Time | Known or Claimed Event |
|---|---|
| 09:05 | A station camera records Orla leaving the records office, carrying a blue ledger case. |
| 10:10 | Orla argues with Ilyan in the greenhouse corridor. |
| 11:20 | Orla meets Edda Vale in the records office. |
| 11:35 | Toma’s freight train departs eastward. |
| 11:50 | The records office door logs an exit under Orla’s authorization. |
| 12:05 | A cargo trolley passes from the civic quarter toward the windworks. Its camera is obscured by steam. |
| 12:30 | A maintenance alarm briefly sounds in Service Throat Three, then is manually silenced. |
| 15:40 | Pera claims to inspect Service Throat Three; he reports no body and no fallen baffle. |
| 16:50 | Edda claims she sees Orla alive, walking toward the windworks. She produces a camera image as proof. |
| 17:20 | Orla’s body is found beneath the fallen baffle. |
| 17:35 | Pera is detained. |

At first, Edda’s photograph appeared decisive. It showed Orla, alive and upright, passing the civic tower. The tower’s shadow lay at the same angle it would have had at 16:50.

Therefore, the reasoning went, Orla must have died after Pera’s 15:40 inspection.

Pera seemed finished.

---

# Three Ordinary Clues

## 1. The Frost on Orla’s Coat

The service throat was open to Nacre’s permanent dayward-to-nightward wind.

That wind carried humid air from the warmer side of the station. In the colder service throat, moisture condensed and froze on every exposed surface. The rime always accumulated on the **dayward-facing side** of objects.

Orla was found on her back, head toward the nightward exit. Her face, chest, and the front of her coat were exposed to the dayward wind.

Yet the thickest rime lay along the back of her coat and beneath her shoulders.

She had spent hours face-down in the throat before being turned over beneath the baffle.

The baffle had not killed her.

It had been placed there afterward.

## 2. The Blue Dust

The floor of the records office was coated with ordinary pale basalt grit. But beneath the filing shelves was a distinctive blue-grey dust: powdered pigment shed by the old water ledgers, which were bound in treated sail-plant fibre.

The same dust was found in the seams of Orla’s boots.

That alone was unsurprising. She had worked in the records office.

But the dust was also found in the wheel grooves of the cargo trolley that had passed toward the windworks at 12:05.

And it was found on the inner cuff of Edda Vale’s left glove.

Edda claimed she had not entered the records office after her morning meeting with Orla.

## 3. The Photograph and the Shadow

Edda’s image showed Orla beside the civic tower, with the tower’s shadow pointing precisely railward.

At 16:50, during the return, the tower’s shadow did point railward.

It had also pointed railward at 09:05.

The image was not a new photograph at all. It was a cropped frame from the station’s public camera record, taken that morning. Edda had removed the identifying border and presented it as evidence of a later sighting.

Inspector Darel confirmed this by comparing the image with the archived camera sequence. In the original frame, behind Orla, a rail porter was carrying a yellow pressure suit. The same porter appeared in Edda’s cropped image, reduced to a sliver of yellow at the edge.

Edda had relied on the returning light to make an old image look current.

---

# Solution

Orla Fen had found evidence that water credits were being diverted from Vesper’s public reserve accounts.

The stolen credits had not gone to Ilyan’s greenhouses or to Toma’s smugglers. They had been routed through a series of maintenance transfers and then sold, indirectly, to private storage firms beyond the station.

The fraud had been organized by Edda Vale.

Pera’s falsified maintenance reports gave Edda a convenient channel through which to disguise the transfers. Pera was guilty of negligence and petty fraud, but he did not know the scale of Edda’s theft.

At 11:20, Orla confronted Edda in the records office.

Edda killed her there, striking her with the brass base of a ledger weight. She then used Orla’s authorization token to create the 11:50 exit record, hoping to suggest that Orla had left the office alive.

At noon, Edda placed Orla’s body on a cargo trolley and pushed it through the steam corridor toward Service Throat Three. The blue ledger dust transferred from Orla’s boots and clothing to the trolley wheels and to Edda’s glove.

She laid Orla face-down in the throat, allowing the dayward wind to frost her back. Later, she turned the body over, released the already-loosened wind-baffle, and arranged the scene to resemble a maintenance failure.

Finally, she produced the old 09:05 camera image and claimed it showed Orla alive at 16:50.

That placed Orla’s supposed death after Pera’s inspection and made him the obvious culprit.

But the frost proved Orla had been exposed in the service throat since shortly after noon. The photograph was old. And only Edda had both the motive to suppress the water records and the knowledge that the returning shadow could make a morning image pass for an evening one.

---

# Final Scene

Edda Vale stood with Inspector Darel in the civic tower’s narrow observation gallery.

Outside, the red star had nearly stopped in the haze. The tower’s shadow stretched toward the rails, thin and black across the pale ground.

“It points the same way,” Edda said.

“Yes,” Darel replied.

Below them, workers were unbolting the fallen baffle from Service Throat Three. The wind moved steadily toward the dark hemisphere, carrying ice crystals past the station lights.

“You made one mistake,” Darel said.

Edda did not ask which one.

“You trusted the light. But on Nacre, light comes back.”

For a while neither of them spoke. Then Edda looked down at the shadow, as if she had expected it to move.

dream_response:
# CASE FILE: THE NEREID OBSERVATORY ANOMALY  
**Classification:** Internal / Level 4  
**Site:** Nereid High-Altitude Observatory, Cerro Viento, Chile  
**Elevation:** 5,184 m  
**Primary instrument:** Asterion Array, 14.2 m adaptive optical telescope  
**Date file opened:** 17 September 2041  
**Compiled by:** Dr. Mara Ilyan, Acting Site Director  
**Status:** Active; external disclosure prohibited pending verification

---

## 1. INCIDENT SUMMARY

At 02:13:08 UTC on 17 September 2041, the Asterion Array recorded an anomalous modulation in the spectrum of **NQ-4721**, a red dwarf catalogued at an estimated distance of 41.3 light-years.

The modulation initially resembled an instrumental calibration artifact: brief, regularly spaced changes in the star’s H-alpha emission line. However, after correction for atmospheric distortion, detector noise, telescope motion, Earth rotation, and barycentric timing, the pattern remained.

The modulation decoded into digital data.

The data was compressed, error-corrected, and encrypted using an obsolete Nereid Observatory archival standard.

After decryption, the first recovered file was a copy of an observatory incident log timestamped **18 hours, 47 minutes, and 12 seconds in the future.**

The incident described an event which subsequently occurred with apparent accuracy.

Since then, eleven further packets have been received.

All contain records of events that, at the moment of reception, had not yet occurred.

The source appears spatially coincident with NQ-4721.

It cannot be NQ-4721.

---

# 2. PERSONNEL ROSTER

### Dr. Mara Ilyan — Acting Site Director  
Age 46. Astrophysicist. Specialist in stellar magnetohydrodynamics.  
Arrived at Nereid: 2038.  
Notes: Formerly married to Elias Venn, deceased 2035. No disciplinary history.

### Dr. Tomas Vale — Signal Analysis Lead  
Age 34. Computational physicist and cryptographer.  
Arrived at Nereid: 2040.  
Notes: Wrote the current archive-indexing system used on site. Has access to all data-storage infrastructure.

### Dr. Sanaa Okafor — Instrumentation Chief  
Age 51. Optical engineer.  
Arrived at Nereid: 2031.  
Notes: Responsible for Asterion Array detector systems, time synchronization, adaptive optics, and calibration lasers.

### Lucía Serrat — Senior Telescope Operator  
Age 29.  
Arrived at Nereid: 2039.  
Notes: First individual to identify apparent nonrandomness in the modulation. Trained in observational astronomy; no advanced cryptographic background.

### Dr. Jian Wen — Theoretical Physics Consultant  
Age 62.  
Arrived at Nereid: 16 September 2041, one day before first packet.  
Notes: Visiting researcher from the Institute for Fundamental Causality, Singapore. His scheduled topic was gravitational lensing near compact stellar remnants.

### Mateo Rojas — Site Systems Technician  
Age 38.  
Arrived at Nereid: 2036.  
Notes: Maintains power, cooling, communications, and physical infrastructure. Born in Calama. One daughter, Ana, age 9.

---

# 3. INITIAL OBSERVATION LOG

## Asterion Array Observation Record  
**Observer:** Lucía Serrat  
**Date:** 17 September 2041  
**Target:** NQ-4721  
**Mode:** High-resolution optical spectroscopy  
**Start:** 02:11:00 UTC

> **02:12:41** — Seeing unusually stable. Adaptive correction holding at 0.011 arcsec residual.  
>
> **02:13:08** — Repeating asymmetry observed in H-alpha absorption profile. Initially suspected detector saturation or atmospheric sodium contamination.  
>
> **02:13:27** — Anomaly persists after reference-star switch.  
>
> **02:14:02** — Pattern repeats every 17.000 seconds, with substructure at 1.000-second intervals.  
>
> **02:15:46** — Asked Dr. Vale to inspect.  
>
> **02:18:19** — Vale says pattern is “too clean to be stellar.”  
>
> **02:21:03** — Telescope pointing check passed. Sky position agrees with catalog coordinates within 0.003 arcsec.  
>
> **02:24:11** — Signal ceases abruptly. Total duration: 1,063 seconds.

---

# 4. INSTRUMENT READOUT: SIGNAL CHARACTERISTICS

## Extract: Dr. Sanaa Okafor’s Technical Report

**Source designation:** NQ-4721 / “Nereid Signal”  
**Observed wavelength region:** 656.281 nm ± 0.017 nm  
**Carrier behavior:** H-alpha line modulation  
**Apparent angular origin:** Within 0.0028 arcsec of NQ-4721’s catalogued position  
**Observed duration:** 1,063.0 s  
**Packet repetition:** 17.000 ± 0.0003 s  
**Polarization:** Predominantly circular, alternating handedness in packet headers  
**Doppler signature:** Consistent with NQ-4721’s radial velocity to within 14 m/s  
**Parallax behavior:** Consistent with a distant astronomical source, not local interference  
**Atmospheric transit behavior:** Consistent with light entering through the telescope aperture

### Notable irregularities

1. The signal’s **packet timing** is phase-locked not to NQ-4721’s rotation, pulsation, orbital motion, or known stellar activity cycle, but to **UTC seconds**.

2. Correcting timestamps from UTC to TAI, TT, and barycentric dynamical time does not remove the one-second packet structure.

3. The signal exhibits a periodic timing drift of **1.74 milliseconds per sidereal day**, increasing monotonically over the observation window.

4. The drift changes sign when the telescope observes NQ-4721 from the opposite side of the meridian.

5. No comparable modulation was found in observations made by:
   - Alto Cumbre Observatory, Argentina  
   - Kerguelen Southern Array, French Southern Territories  
   - The *Orpheus* orbital telescope  

6. The signal is absent when Nereid is clouded over, even though its radio-frequency systems remain operational.

> **Okafor’s handwritten note, attached to report:**  
> “If this is a transmission from the star, it knows where our telescope is. If it is not from the star, it is doing an exceptionally convincing imitation of starlight.”

---

# 5. DECODED PACKET 01

**Received:** 17 September 2041, 02:13:08 UTC  
**Header timestamp:** 17 September 2041, 21:00:20 UTC  
**Temporal offset:** +18:47:12  
**File type:** `NEREID_INCIDENT_LOG.v6`  
**Integrity:** SHA-512 hash valid under current Nereid archival protocol

```text
INCIDENT LOG 2041-09-17 / 21:00 UTC
AUTHOR: M. ROJAS
CATEGORY: MINOR FACILITY HAZARD

At approximately 20:57 local site time, coolant line 3B ruptured
in the lower instrument gallery. Initial cause appears to be a failed
pressure gasket in the north manifold.

Dr. Okafor sustained a laceration to the left palm while shutting the
manual isolation valve. Injury treated on site. No loss of telescope
cooling; Array entered safe mode for 11 minutes.

Important: the rupture occurred immediately after I reset breaker panel
D-4. The panel had tripped during an unexplained transient load spike.
Recommend electrical inspection before reactivation.

Logged 21:00:20 UTC.
```

### Verification

At 20:57:44 UTC, a coolant line in the lower gallery ruptured.

At 20:58:11 UTC, Dr. Okafor cut her left palm on the isolation-valve housing.

At 20:59:02 UTC, Mateo Rojas reset breaker panel D-4.

The packet had not merely described the event generally. Its wording, timestamps, and author metadata matched the log Rojas wrote afterward, including a misspelling in the word “reactivation” that was present in both files.

The future log’s file hash was identical to the later-created original.

---

# 6. STAFF TRANSCRIPT: FIRST DISCUSSION

**Time:** 21:17 UTC, 17 September  
**Location:** Operations Room  
**Recording:** Automatic safety system transcription; lightly edited for clarity

**ILYAN:** Nobody says “time travel” until we’ve exhausted every failure mode we understand.

**VALE:** It had the hash before the file existed.

**ILYAN:** It had a hash matching a file that later existed on our system. That is not the same sentence.

**OKAFOR:** The line burst because Mateo reset D-4. The file said he would reset D-4.

**ROJAS:** I reset it because it tripped. I would have reset it regardless.

**VALE:** Would you? If you had not received a report saying you were going to?

**ROJAS:** …I don’t know.

**WEN:** That is the first useful thing anyone has said.

**SERRAT:** What do you mean?

**WEN:** A prediction can alter the event it predicts. If the event still occurs, either the prediction has no causal influence—or it is part of the cause.

**ILYAN:** Dr. Wen, I did not invite a philosopher.

**WEN:** No. You invited someone to speak about lensing around objects with pathological causal structure.

**[Silence: 8.4 seconds.]**

**VALE:** You knew something was here?

**WEN:** I knew Nereid was built near an unusual gravimetric anomaly. I did not know about this signal.

**ILYAN:** You will provide every document you have.

**WEN:** Of course.

---

# 7. DECODED PACKET 02

**Received:** 17 September 2041, 22:06:41 UTC  
**Header timestamp:** 18 September 2041, 16:53:53 UTC  
**Temporal offset:** +18:47:12  
**File type:** `PERSONAL_NOTE_MILYAN.txt`  
**Integrity:** Valid

```text
I have asked them to stop calling it the star.

It is not the star. The star is forty-one light-years away. Whatever is
happening, it is using the star like a mask.

Tomas thinks the packets come from a future copy of our archival server.
Sanaa thinks that is impossible, which is fair, but she has started
checking the thermal logs every six minutes.

Jian has finally told us about the borehole.

He says there is something below the old foundation: not a black hole,
not exactly, and not natural in any ordinary sense. He says the relevant
word is "shortcut," but he will not say between what and what.

Tomorrow at 07:12 we will lower a gravimeter into Borehole 7.

I do not want to do this. But Lucía says the next packet will arrive
before then.

She is right.

—M.
```

### Verification status at time of compilation

At the time Packet 02 was decoded, Dr. Ilyan had not written this note.

At 07:12 UTC on 18 September, after extended discussion, the staff lowered a gravimeter into Borehole 7.

At 16:53:53 UTC, Dr. Ilyan wrote the note exactly as received.

She later stated that she attempted to alter its wording. She deleted the phrase “not exactly” and replaced it with “not remotely.” When she saved the file, the original phrase appeared.

Dr. Vale verified that the keyboard input log recorded Ilyan typing “not remotely.”

No remote access event was found.

---

# 8. EXCERPT: BOREHOLE 7 SURVEY HISTORY

**Original site-survey document, 2028**  
**Classification at time:** Commercial confidential  
**Released to Nereid staff by Dr. Wen on 18 September 2041**

```text
GEOLOGICAL ANOMALY REPORT: CERRO VIENTO, SECTOR N-7

At depth 183.6 m, drilling encountered material with the following
properties:

- Density cannot be determined by conventional gravimetric methods.
- Core extraction attempts failed; drill head temperature fell by 61°C
  during contact despite increased mechanical energy input.
- Local gravitational gradient varies with sidereal orientation.
- Radar, ultrasound, and muon tomography return mutually inconsistent
  boundaries.

The anomaly occupies an estimated volume of 4–11 cubic meters.

Recommendation: suspend extraction. Reclassify region as unsuitable for
deep-foundation construction.

Addendum, 2030:
A funding authority has requested consideration of the location for an
astronomical facility. Proposed foundation plan avoids direct penetration
of Sector N-7 by 38 m.

Addendum, 2032:
Foundation plan revised. Telescope pier located 11.4 m east of original
recommendation due to bedrock stability.
```

### Margin annotation, author unknown

> “The object is not beneath the telescope. The telescope is beneath its line of sight.”

---

# 9. DECODED PACKET 03

**Received:** 18 September 2041, 03:34:00 UTC  
**Header timestamp:** 18 September 2041, 22:21:12 UTC  
**Temporal offset:** +18:47:12  
**File type:** `OBSERVATORY_AUDIO_TRANSCRIPT.txt`  
**Integrity:** Valid

```text

[22:18:10] ROJAS: It's a gravimeter, Lucía.
```

```text
PLEASE DO NOT LOWER IT AGAIN.
```

---

# 10. PRIVATE NOTE: LUCÍA SERRAT

**Recovered from an unsent encrypted message draft.**  
**Timestamp:** 18 September 2041, 04:02 UTC  
**Recipient field:** `mama`

```text
Mamá,

I know I told you not to worry if I stopped answering. That was supposed
to be a joke.

Something is happening here. I cannot explain it without sounding ill.
We are receiving files from tomorrow. Not predictions. They are actual
files, with things in them that people have not decided yet.

The worst part is that I think some of them are trying to make the files
wrong. Tomas keeps saying that if the future can send us a record, then
we should be able to change it. Dr. Wen says that might be exactly what
the record is for.

There is a message in the last packet that nobody else has noticed.

Not in the text. In the gaps.

Every packet has 17 blocks. The first letters of the error-correction
labels spell the same thing if you read them backward.

I haven't told them because I don't know what it means.

It says:

M A R A  I S  T H E  K E Y

I don't know whether that means Mara can stop this.

Or whether it means she starts it.
```

---

# 11. PACKET STRUCTURE ANALYSIS

## Dr. Tomas Vale, Preliminary Cryptographic Report

Each received packet contains:

- 17 transmission blocks  
- 1 header block  
- 15 data blocks  
- 1 parity block  

The error-correction labels appear to be arbitrary hexadecimal strings. However, their initial characters, read in reverse packet order, form the phrase:

> **MARA IS THE KEY**

The result is reproducible across Packets 01–03.

### Secondary observation

The payload encryption uses an archive key derived from the following inputs:

1. Current observatory date  
2. Telescope pointing coordinates  
3. A 256-bit site-specific salt  
4. A biometric value associated with Dr. Mara Ilyan’s retinal scan

The archive system does not currently use biometric keys.

No current or legacy Nereid software module contains the relevant derivation routine.

### Vale’s conclusion

> “Whoever encoded these packets either has future access to our systems, or has reconstructed them perfectly. The inclusion of Mara’s biometric signature is not decorative. It is required to decrypt the messages.”

---

# 12. REDACTED COMMUNICATION: INSTITUTE FOR FUNDAMENTAL CAUSALITY

**Sender:** Dr. Jian Wen  
**Recipient:** Prof. Elise Arendt  
**Date:** 11 September 2041  
**Recovered from Wen’s personal terminal**  
**Status:** Partial; portions removed by sender-side encryption

```text
Elise,

I have accepted the Nereid invitation.

The survey coordinates overlap with the Viento anomaly, though the
observatory staff do not appear to have been told the whole history.
The 2028 data is worse than I remembered. The object’s effective stress-
energy tensor is [REDACTED], but the surrounding metric admits null
geodesics with a return interval of approximately [REDACTED].

If the object is what I think it is, it does not create a conventional
closed timelike curve. It creates something more selective: an optical
channel whose endpoints may be separated in both distance and time.

The troubling part is its orientation.

One end appears to point toward NQ-4721.

The other appears to point toward the site where Nereid was later built.

That wording is deliberate. “Later built.”

I do not know whether the anomaly predates the observatory or whether
the observatory is among the conditions that produce it.

I know how that sounds.

—J.
```

# 13. TIMELINE OF CONFIRMED EVENTS

All times UTC.

---

# 14. INCIDENT REPORT: LOSS OF TARGET

**Observer:** Lucía Serrat  
**Time:** 19 September, 01:20 UTC

```text
NQ-4721 did not set. It did not move behind cloud. It did not dim.

It was simply no longer where it had been.

The star field around it remained. Every comparison star was where it
should have been. But at the exact coordinates of NQ-4721 there was a
black point, smaller than the diffraction limit and darker than the
background sky.

Not empty. Dark.

When I shifted the telescope 0.01 arcsec east, NQ-4721 was present again.

When I shifted it back, it vanished.

This is not an occultation. Whatever we are looking at is narrower than
the telescope’s pointing resolution and somehow knows when we are
looking directly at it.

At 01:20:17, the black point produced a single pulse.

No data packet was recovered.

The pulse duration was 0.004 seconds.

In that time, every display in the control room showed the same line:

    YOU HAVE ALREADY RECEIVED THE WARNING.
```

---

# 15. WITHHELD PACKET 04: PARTIAL LEAK

Packet 04 was restricted by Dr. Ilyan. At 03:07 UTC, Lucía Serrat copied a portion of it into the telescope operator’s scratch directory before access was revoked.

Recovered excerpt:

```text
NEREID FATALITY REPORT
DATE: 19 SEPTEMBER 2041
TIME OF INCIDENT: 19:52:31 UTC

DECEASED: [REDACTED]

CAUSE OF DEATH: Exposure to rapid decompression and hypoxia following
structural failure in the primary mirror service chamber.

CONTRIBUTING FACTORS:
- Manual override of observatory safety interlocks
- Activation of [REDACTED]
- Presence of unauthorized personnel in restricted chamber
- Failure to heed prior warning

FINAL NOTE:
The recovered transmission record indicates that the deceased was aware
of this report before the incident occurred.
```

The identity of the deceased is absent from the copied excerpt.

The full packet is encrypted under a key that requires Dr. Ilyan’s retinal signature.

Dr. Ilyan has refused to decrypt it in the presence of the others.

---

# 16. OBSERVATIONS AVAILABLE TO INVESTIGATORS

The following facts are currently considered reliable:

1. The packets accurately reproduce files created approximately **18 hours, 47 minutes, 12 seconds later**.

2. The packets are received as light apparently arriving from NQ-4721, 41.3 light-years away.

3. The effect is visible only from Nereid and only through the telescope’s optical path.

4. The signal timing contains an Earth-rotation-dependent drift inconsistent with an ordinary distant transmitter.

5. The buried anomaly beneath Cerro Viento has unusual gravitational properties and may support an optical path with nonstandard temporal behavior.

6. Packet encryption requires Mara Ilyan’s biometric signature despite no such feature existing in the present archival system.

7. The packets contain embedded text: **MARA IS THE KEY.**

8. A fatal event is predicted for **19:52:31 UTC**, less than nineteen hours after the latest known packet.

9. At least one individual at Nereid has withheld information.

10. The warning in Packet 03—“PLEASE DO NOT LOWER IT AGAIN”—may have originated from an unknown sender, from the future staff, from the anomaly itself, or from some other source entirely.

---

## OPEN QUESTIONS

- Is NQ-4721 truly involved, or merely being used as an apparent source direction?  
- Why does the temporal offset remain exactly constant despite changes in telescope position and Earth’s motion?  
- Why is Mara Ilyan required to decrypt the packets?  
- Who created the future archive system?  
- What is the buried object beneath Borehole 7?  
- Why did Dr. Wen arrive one day before the first signal?  
- Who is named in the withheld fatality report?  
- Can the predicted death be prevented—or would attempting to prevent it be the event that causes it?  
- Most importantly: if these records come from the future, why are they being sent at all?

dream_response:
By the time Nella was thirty-two, she had learned not to trust photographs.

Photographs were heavy with accuracy. A good one could pin a teacup to a saucer or keep a dead aunt from drifting through the ceiling at her own funeral. The municipal archives, where Nella worked, were built mostly underground for this reason. Three floors of certified birthmarks, school reports, dental molds, and witness statements held the building down against the accumulated inaccuracies of the city.

Aboveground, people preferred softer things.

They called the law Correction, as if it were a minor inconvenience, like rain. The world had a strong opinion about being described properly. Every false detail remembered about a person gave them a little lift.

A mistaken eye color might make someone’s heels skim the pavement.

A false childhood anecdote could bring a grown man level with a second-story window.

A beloved national myth could require a chain.

Nella’s job was to compare old recollections with records and issue ballast recommendations. She wrote things like:

> SUBJECT: Iona Bell  
> COMMON ERROR: “Hated pears.”  
> VERIFIED: Ate pears daily until age seventy-one.  
> ESTIMATED LIFT: 4.2 kilograms.  
> RECOMMENDATION: Family should cease saying “Aunt Iona hated pears” at gatherings.

It was tedious, precise work, and Nella liked it. Truth was neat. Truth had corners.

Her mother had not liked truth nearly as much.

“Your father could whistle through a closed door,” her mother used to say.

“He taught me to ride a bicycle with one hand,” Nella would add.

“And he wore that dreadful red shirt.”

“The one with the white gulls.”

“Not gulls,” her mother would say, smiling. “Little boats.”

“Gulls.”

“Boats.”

They had this argument every year on Nella’s birthday, cheerful as a hymn.

Her father had disappeared when she was six. Officially, he had “experienced an uncontained elevation event.” This was the polite phrase. In practice, he had floated out through the kitchen window on a Thursday afternoon and been carried east over the canal, waving one arm while her mother screamed instructions about his boots.

Nella remembered none of that.

What she remembered was the bicycle.

The lane behind their house smelled of wet brick and fennel. Her father ran behind her, one hand on the little metal rack above the back wheel. His red shirt flashed between the hedges. He kept shouting, “Pedal, Nell! Pedal, you marvelous disaster!”

Then he let go.

She did not fall.

For the rest of her life, this had been the brightest room in her mind: the warm evening, the wobble of handlebars, her father laughing as she went free.

After her mother died, Nella found a tin box beneath the flour bin.

It contained receipts, a house key from a house they had never lived in, and a municipal correction notice with her father’s name.

The paper was brittle. The ink had bled slightly at the folds.

> SUBJECT: Tomas Venn  
> VERIFIED CHARACTERISTICS:  
> — Brown eyes  
> — Left-handed  
> — Never owned a bicycle  
> — Never wore red  
> — No children present at incident  
>   
> OBSERVED ASCENT: 19 April, 3:16 p.m.  
> CAUSAL FACTORS: Repeated inaccurate recollections, primary source unknown.

Nella read it three times.

Then she sat at the kitchen table until the afternoon light climbed the walls.

Never owned a bicycle.

No children present.

Her father’s eyes had been brown, then. She had always pictured them blue. Sea-blue. Impossible, shining blue. She remembered the gulls on his shirt—no, the boats—and the bicycle bell, and his broad hand on her back.

By dusk, the old tin box had become light enough to tremble.

Nella put a dictionary on it.

The next morning she went to the archives and requested the sealed files from 19 April, twenty-six years ago. Her supervisor, Mr. Prawl, peered at the form.

“Family matter?” he asked.

“Yes.”

“Best to leave those alone,” he said. “Old memories are like old boilers. You inspect them and suddenly everyone’s on the roof.”

But he stamped the request.

The file was thin. Her father had worked at a glassworks. He had been accused of falsifying safety numbers after an explosion injured seven people. There was a warrant. There were witness statements saying he had begun to lift in the weeks beforehand.

His wife, Elian Venn, had supplied most of the inaccuracies.

She had told neighbors he had blue eyes.

She had told the grocer he played the trumpet.

She had told the landlord he was allergic to onions, that he had once shaken hands with the mayor, that he collected porcelain frogs, that he had a limp in the rain.

She had told little Nella a story about a red shirt, a bicycle, a lane behind a house they had never owned.

Each error had lifted him.

Not much at first. A few ounces. Then pounds.

On the day of the warrant, he had risen from the kitchen floor, knocking a spoon from the counter with his shoulder. Elian had opened the window.

There was a final note in her handwriting, attached to the file.

> I’m sorry. He was not a good man in all ways. But he was your father, and he was frightened, and I could not bear to let them take him down.

Nella sat beneath the archive’s low stone ceiling.

All around her, shelves held the facts that kept people on the ground.

She thought of her mother correcting gulls into boats, year after year. Not because she cared about birds. Not because she was teasing.

Because she was maintaining the lie.

Because the lie had become a rope, and the rope had become wind.

That evening, Nella took her mother’s tin box to the canal. She set it on the railing and removed the dictionary.

It rose at once, slowly and with great dignity.

Inside were the red-shirt story, the false bicycle, the blue eyes. All those years of careful invention, compacted into paper and memory and the weightless space between what happened and what could be borne.

The box drifted upward above the water.

Nella could have called it back down. She knew the correct procedure. She could recite the facts: brown eyes, left-handed, no bicycle, no child on the lane.

Instead, she watched it climb.

A gull landed on the railing beside her and looked suspiciously at the ascending tin.

“Boats,” Nella said.

The gull gave her a hard, professional stare.

“Fine,” she said. “Gulls.”

The box dipped, almost imperceptibly, then continued into the evening sky.

dream_prompt:
Write a self-contained “found document” from an alternate world: **The Municipal Handbook for the Care and Feeding of Small, Urban Dragons**, 7th edition, published by a weary city bureaucracy.

Include practical regulations, marginal notes from frustrated inspectors, one alarming historical incident, a few oddly touching details about dragon behavior, and evidence that the handbook’s authors are trying very hard not to acknowledge that the dragons may understand every word.

Make it funny, precise, atmospheric, and gradually a little melancholy. Do not explain the premise outside the document.
dream_response:
# THE MUNICIPAL HANDBOOK  
## FOR THE CARE AND FEEDING OF SMALL, URBAN DRAGONS  
### Seventh Edition, Revised and Corrected  
Issued by the Office of Sanitation, Vermin, Smoke, and Other Civic Concerns  
City of Bellwether  
Approved by Council Resolution 44-B, excepting Appendix IX, which remains “under review.”

**Property of the Municipality.**  
Not to be stored near open flame, brass fittings, loose coins, or windows accessible to juveniles of the genus *Draco civicus*.

---

### PREFACE TO THE SEVENTH EDITION

The purpose of this handbook is to assist householders, landlords, chimney sweeps, night watchmen, market inspectors, tram conductors, and other unwilling custodians in the humane management of small urban dragons.

For the purposes of municipal regulation, a “small urban dragon” is defined as any scaled, winged, semi-domesticated fire-producing animal:

1. Not exceeding thirty-eight inches from snout to tail base;
2. Capable of fitting through a standard coal chute;
3. Known to nest in roofs, boiler rooms, abandoned piano cases, or beneath the warmest available tenant;
4. Possessing no more than three documented incidents of malicious structural ignition in a calendar year.

Dragons exceeding the above dimensions must be reported to the Department of Unusual Livestock within forty-eight hours, unless reporting would require approaching the dragon, in which case the resident should report from a safe public house.

This edition incorporates updated feeding guidance, revised smoke-ventilation standards, and corrections to the commonly repeated falsehood that dragons “prefer solitude.”

They do not prefer solitude.

They prefer to be asked twice.

— **M. Pell, Acting Deputy Commissioner**  
Office of Sanitation, Vermin, Smoke, and Other Civic Concerns

*[Inspector’s marginal note, in pencil: “That sentence was not in the approved draft.”]*

---

## PART I: IDENTIFICATION AND REGISTRATION

### 1.1 Common Varieties

Most city residents will encounter one of the following:

- **The Gutter Saffron:** yellow-brown, quick-tempered, fond of rain spouts and fried fish.
- **The Brick Red:** broad-headed, heat-efficient, generally suitable for older buildings with poor insulation.
- **The Blue Chimney:** pale blue-gray, quiet, prone to collecting buttons and watching snowfall.
- **The Coalback:** black-scaled, stocky, often mistaken for a sooty cat until it sneezes.
- **The Municipal Green:** rare, illegal to breed, universally claimed to “belong to someone else.”

Under no circumstances should a resident identify a dragon by attempting to lift it. Dragons are deceptively dense, and the act is considered discourteous by both parties.

### 1.2 Registration

All dragons residing within city limits for more than fourteen consecutive nights must be registered at the nearest ward office.

Owners, custodians, and persons who insist the dragon “just comes around sometimes” must provide:

- Name of dragon, if known;
- Approximate age;
- Coloration;
- Principal sleeping location;
- Number of household spoons missing since its arrival;
- Whether it has shown an interest in newspapers, music boxes, family correspondence, or the conduct of private conversations.

The final item exists solely for record-keeping and should not be interpreted as implying unusual comprehension.

*[Inspector’s note: “Do not say ‘spells aloud’ while filling out this form.”]*

---

## PART II: HOUSING REQUIREMENTS

### 2.1 Nesting Space

A healthy dragon requires:

- One enclosed sleeping area;
- One warm surface;
- One object it may consider “its own,” preferably nonflammable;
- Adequate ventilation;
- A route of exit that does not pass through the kitchen pantry.

Appropriate nesting materials include wool scraps, old blankets, chimney ash, corks, broken clockwork, and unimportant official papers.

Inappropriate materials include gunpowder, lamp oil, dried holly, railway schedules, and any document marked **FINAL NOTICE**.

### 2.2 Hearth Access

Dragons may be permitted supervised access to household hearths during cold weather. Fire grates must be fitted with a dragon guard of no less than two inches in height.

Do not lock a dragon inside a cold grate as punishment.

This prohibition was added after the North Market Incident, during which twelve dragons, acting without apparent coordination, extinguished every domestic fire in the district on the same evening.

No explanation has been established.

*[Handwritten beneath: “They were not ‘acting without coordination.’” Initials illegible.]*

### 2.3 Windows

Windows at dragon height should be secured with wire mesh.

This is not primarily to prevent escape. Most dragons can open a latched window if sufficiently motivated.

The mesh is intended to discourage them from sitting on exterior ledges during thunderstorms, where they are prone to become wet, offended, and difficult to retrieve.

---

## PART III: FEEDING

### 3.1 Approved Diet

Small urban dragons should be fed twice daily, with a supplemental evening portion during winter or periods of emotional disturbance.

Suitable foods include:

- Cooked mutton;
- Eggs, preferably warm;
- Root vegetables roasted in their skins;
- Sardines;
- Charred bread crusts;
- Bones with marrow;
- Small quantities of pepper;
- Copper pennies minted before the reign of King Aldren III.

Modern pennies are nutritionally empty and should not be offered except as toys.

A dragon must never be fed raw coal. This practice produces excessive smoke, stubbornness, and a breath odor detectable through two closed doors.

### 3.2 Treats

Treats may be used to encourage desirable behavior, including:

- Returning from rooftops;
- Relinquishing stolen cutlery;
- Not sleeping in laundry baskets;
- Refraining from roasting the postman;
- Allowing children to wear scarves indoors.

Treats should not be offered immediately after undesirable behavior, even if the dragon appears unusually sorry.

They nearly always appear unusually sorry.

### 3.3 Feeding Etiquette

Place food in a low ceramic dish and step back at least three paces.

Do not stare while the dragon eats. This is rude.

Do not remove food once offered. This is ruder.

Do not say, “You know perfectly well what you did.”

No finding has been made as to whether dragons understand this phrase. Nevertheless, its use has correlated strongly with overturned furniture.

---

## PART IV: FIRE SAFETY AND PUBLIC CONDUCT

### 4.1 Sneezing

A dragon sneeze may produce sparks at a range of six to fourteen feet. Warning signs include twitching nostrils, a stiffened tail, and a look of profound betrayal.

In the event of a sneeze:

1. Remove curtains.
2. Remove yourself.
3. If possible, remove the dragon from proximity to upholstered items.
4. Do not shout. It is already embarrassed.

### 4.2 Public Transport

Dragons may travel on trams provided they are contained in an approved carrier, basket, satchel, or coat pocket.

They must not occupy seats during peak hours.

They must not enter the conductor’s booth.

They must not, under any circumstances, be allowed to “help” with tickets.

Following the incident of 11 Frostfall, when three dragons allegedly altered the destination cards on Route 6, all conductors are instructed to count passengers rather than rely on posted signage.

### 4.3 Roof Roaming

Dragons are permitted on roofs between sunset and sunrise, except during municipal elections, fireworks displays, or chimney-cleaning operations.

Residents should expect a dragon to return before dawn unless it has found something shiny, encountered another dragon, or heard music.

If it has not returned by breakfast, leave the window unlatched and place a warm egg on the sill.

Do not call its name through the streets.

This will only encourage neighborhood children, and, in some cases, the dragon.

---

## PART V: THE HARBOR STREET CONFLAGRATION OF 1889

This section is included because residents continue to cite the Harbor Street Conflagration as a reason to demand total dragon removal, despite the fact that total removal has repeatedly proven impractical, expensive, and emotionally disruptive to both citizens and animals.

On the night of 3 November 1889, a warehouse containing lamp oil, imported rugs, two pianos, and an unregistered population of nineteen small urban dragons caught fire.

Initial reports blamed the dragons.

Subsequent reports blamed faulty wiring.

Later reports blamed the warehouse owner, Mr. Hollis Vane, who had attempted to heat the building by placing portable braziers beneath dragon nests in order to “improve output.”

The dragons escaped through the upper windows. Witnesses reported that several made repeated return flights into the burning structure.

They emerged carrying, among other items:

- A crate of account books;
- Three sleeping cats;
- One infant, uninjured;
- A fireman’s helmet;
- An entire rug, still smoldering;
- Mr. Vane’s trousers.

The final item was deposited in the harbor.

No municipal position is taken on the matter of intent.

The warehouse was destroyed. Two adjoining buildings were damaged. No human fatalities occurred.

Of the nineteen dragons, eighteen were recovered.

The nineteenth, a blue chimney dragon known as Morrow, was observed for several years thereafter on the old Customs House roof. Dockworkers left fish for it. It declined all attempts at capture.

When the Customs House was demolished in 1902, Morrow was not found.

*[A note in different ink: “He was found. Mrs. Saye kept him in her bakery until 1907. Please stop printing this.”]*

---

## PART VI: HEALTH, GRIEF, AND THE AGING DRAGON

### 6.1 Signs of Illness

Contact a licensed dragon veterinarian if the animal displays:

- Cold scales;
- Persistent wheezing;
- Refusal of eggs;
- Unusual silence;
- Failure to hoard;
- Sleeping in an exposed location;
- Repeated attention to an empty chair, hearthstone, or doorway.

The last condition may also occur after a household death, a tenant’s departure, or the removal of a familiar piece of furniture.

It is not recommended to move a dragon’s preferred chair without allowing it time to inspect the replacement.

### 6.2 Old Age

Urban dragons commonly live between forty and seventy years, depending on diet, housing, soot exposure, and whether anyone has loved them improperly.

Older dragons lose heat slowly. They may prefer windowsills to hearths, and may no longer fly above the third story. Their hoards become less valuable and more specific: a child’s shoe buckle, a cracked spectacles case, a key that opens nothing presently known.

Do not discard these objects.

A dragon may spend an entire afternoon arranging them in sunlight.

Residents are advised to sit nearby without making a fuss.

### 6.3 Death and Disposal

Upon a dragon’s death, notify the ward office.

Do not place the body with ordinary refuse.

Do not attempt cremation.

Do not say, “It was only an animal,” especially in a building with other dragons present.

Municipal collection can be arranged within two business days. Until then, cover the body with a clean cloth and keep the room warm.

If there are other dragons in the household, permit them to approach. They may circle the body, leave small objects beside it, or breathe a single spark into the cloth. This is normal behavior and does not constitute a fire hazard if proper precautions have been followed.

Most will leave before morning.

Some will remain.

---

### FINAL REMINDER TO HOUSEHOLDERS

Small urban dragons are not toys, appliances, vermin, or reliable sources of heat.

They are licensed domestic hazards deserving of patience, regular meals, and the reasonable presumption that they have heard nothing whatsoever of importance.

Keep records. Mend your chimney. Count your spoons.

And if, in the deepest part of winter, you wake to find a small warm weight sleeping against your feet, do not move too quickly.

There are fewer of them every year.

dream_response:
**From _Elements of Apophatics_, Sixth Collegiate Edition**  
*Department of Negative Phenomena, University of Aster Vale*  
*Recovered from the personal papers of Dr. Ilyan Vey, Chair of Applied Absence*  

---

### 1. Definition of the Field

**Apophatics** is the measurement and classification of events which exert detectable consequences despite not occurring.

The elementary object of study is the **absence-event**: a sufficiently expected occurrence whose non-occurrence leaves a measurable deformation in its surrounding system.

Examples include:

- a door not opened at its customary hour;
- a bell not rung because its ringer died before dawn;
- an expected letter never written;
- a child not born into a family prepared for one;
- a sentence abandoned before its final word.

It is essential to distinguish apophatics from ordinary probability theory. Probability concerns what *may* happen. Apophatics concerns what *failed to happen after becoming structurally necessary*.

A coin which lands heads creates no absence of tails; the result was contingent. But when the East Gate has opened every morning for three hundred and twelve years, and on the three hundred and thirteenth morning remains shut, the unopened gate acquires what is called **negative mass**.

Negative mass is not metaphorical. It bends clocks, disturbs animal migration, and, in concentrated forms, causes witnesses to recall conversations that never took place.

> **Marginal note, in Vey’s hand:**  
> “Students are warned against calling this ‘regret physics.’ Regret is private. An absence-event is public, measurable, and occasionally hungry.”

---

### 2. Units and Instruments

The standard unit of absence is the **hollow** (h), defined as the residual causal pressure produced by one socially inevitable event withheld for one second.

Thus, if the noon whistle at the Tern Foundry fails to sound for ten minutes, and its sounding was expected by all workers within hearing range, the resulting absence may be measured in hollow-seconds.

Small absences dissipate harmlessly. Larger ones form persistent structures called **lacunae**.

A lacuna may be detected by means of a **nullometer**, an instrument composed of:

1. a sealed mercury clock,
2. a cage containing six migratory moths,
3. a spool of black thread suspended over a polished silver plate,
4. one object whose normal use has been deliberately prevented.

The object is called the **anchor**.

When a lacuna is present, the clock loses time, the moths orient toward sealed surfaces, and the black thread forms loops whose number corresponds approximately to the local absence density.

> **Figure 2: Standard Nullometric Arrangement**  
> Imagine a circular table viewed from above. At its center rests a closed brass box containing the anchor: a key that fits no lock currently in the room. To the north stands the mercury clock, its hands visible through a glass dome. To the east is the moth cage. To the south lies the silver plate, across which the black thread hangs from a small iron hook.  
>   
> Under normal conditions, the thread’s reflection appears directly beneath it. In the presence of a lacuna, the reflection curves away, as though avoiding an invisible body on the plate.

---

### 3. The Principle of Deferred Consequence

The foundational law of the discipline was established by Mother Orsol in 441 A.V., during her studies of villages evacuated before the Salt Fever.

> **Orsol’s Law of Deferred Consequence**  
> For every absence-event \(A\) that is expected by a sufficiently connected population, there exists a compensating region \(R\) in which the consequences of \(A\) accumulate without cause.  
>   
> Formally:
>
> \[
> \int_R C(x,t)\,dx = \kappa \cdot E(A) \cdot \Delta t
> \]
>
> where:
>
> - \(C(x,t)\) is uncaused consequence density;
> - \(E(A)\) is the expectation weight of the omitted event;
> - \(\Delta t\) is the duration of the omission;
> - \(\kappa\) is the local sympathy constant.

In simpler language: **what does not happen must happen somewhere else, in pieces.**

If a wedding is canceled, flowers may bloom out of season in an unrelated district. If a train fails to arrive, strangers may wait for one another in towns they had no intention of visiting. If a declaration of war is never made, its casualties may emerge as inexplicable accidents over several generations.

The law has been criticized for its moral implications. This is an error. Nature is not said to be moral because rivers obey gravity.

> **Marginal note:**  
> “Orsol wrote ‘somewhere else.’ We have treated that phrase as geographical for eighty years. I no longer believe she meant geography.”

---

## FIELD REPORT 19-B  
### The Unentered Room at Bellwether House

**Investigator:** Dr. Ilyan Vey  
**Date:** 17 Frostwane, 612 A.V.  
**Site:** Former residence of the Bellwether family, Lower Aster Vale  
**Classification:** Domestic lacuna; suspected persistent negative mass

#### Background

Bellwether House has contained, since its construction in 529 A.V., a room on the third floor which no resident is known to have entered.

The room is not locked. Its door opens freely. Household records indicate that each generation intended to use it: nursery, guest chamber, archive, infirmary, and, in the final family registry, “the blue room.”

Yet all plans were abandoned before execution.

Servants dusted the corridor outside it. Children were forbidden to touch its handle. Two Bellwether heirs reported dreams in which they had slept there, despite never having crossed the threshold. Both later described the room identically: blue wallpaper, a cracked window, and a chair facing the wall.

The room was opened for the first time by municipal surveyors in 604 A.V.

It contained no wallpaper, no furniture, no window, and no floor.

There was a doorway, then a volume of darkness extending downward beyond the foundations of the house.

The surveyors sealed the door.

In 611 A.V., after reports of missing time among neighboring residents, the Department authorized apophatic examination.

---

### 4. Experimental Procedure

Three anchors were prepared:

- **Anchor A:** a nursery rattle, never given to the infant for whom it had been purchased;
- **Anchor B:** a guest-book signed by every living Bellwether except the youngest daughter, Mara, who disappeared in 603 A.V.;
- **Anchor C:** a brass house key found in the pocket of the family patriarch after his death. It fit the third-floor door, though the door had never been locked.

Three nullometers were installed:

- **Station One:** at the threshold;
- **Station Two:** in the corridor, twelve paces away;
- **Station Three:** in the dining room beneath the room.

No human observer entered the chamber. This restriction followed standard protocol after the Gannet Pier Incident, in which an intern was found standing inside an unbuilt lighthouse, eight miles from shore.

Observations began at 23:00.

#### Initial Results

At 23:07, Station One’s thread lifted from the silver plate without wind.

At 23:11, all six moths at Station Two faced the sealed door of the house rather than the third-floor room.

At 23:19, Station Three’s mercury clock began running backward at a rate of forty-one seconds per minute.

At 23:26, the guest-book opened itself to a blank page.

At 23:31, a child’s voice was heard from within the chamber. It said:

> “You may come in now.”

No personnel moved.

At 23:34, the voice repeated the sentence, but with one alteration:

> “You already came in.”

> **Marginal note:**  
> “I told the team this was auditory pareidolia. This was a lie. I heard Mara Bellwether. I had heard her once before, in a different house, when I was nine.”

---

### 5. Results of the Bellwether Experiment

At 23:40, the nullometers ceased registering local absence density.

Instead, all three began recording a uniform value of 1.00 hollow per second.

This value persisted irrespective of location. Station One was at the door, Station Two in the corridor, Station Three two floors below; their readings remained identical to six decimal places.

The obvious interpretation was instrument failure.

To test this, the team carried Station Three into the street.

Its reading did not change.

A second team carried a portable nullometer to the railway station, three miles away. Its reading was likewise 1.00 h/s.

At 00:03, messages arrived by telegraph from five Department observatories across the continent. Their instruments had begun displaying the same value at precisely 23:40.

The Department’s official statement attributed the event to a synchronization defect in the standard mercury assemblies.

This explanation is untenable. Several observatories used water clocks, pendulum clocks, and—in the case of North Serein—a trained blind dog conditioned to bark when absence density exceeded 0.2 h/s.

All reported the same reading.

At 00:17, the black threads at all three stations formed the same shape.

It resembled a handwritten sentence.

> **Diagram 7: Thread Configuration Recorded at Bellwether House**  
> The thread begins at the iron hook in the upper left. It descends in a straight line, then curls sharply right into a narrow loop—the form of a capital “I.” It continues in uneven, trembling strokes. The full phrase, confirmed by photography, reads:
>
> **I AM THE ROOM YOU DID NOT ENTER.**

---

### 6. Preliminary Interpretation

The conventional model assumes that absence-events occur *within* the world and produce compensatory consequences elsewhere in it.

Bellwether House suggests a more disturbing alternative.

Perhaps an unentered room does not merely accumulate the consequences of entry deferred.

Perhaps it accumulates the people who would have entered it.

Perhaps every refusal, interruption, death, hesitation, lost key, changed train, and unspoken sentence is not erased. Perhaps it is admitted into another volume: one constructed entirely from withheld arrivals.

Under this model, lacunae are not voids.

They are habitats.

> **Marginal note:**  
> “This would explain the child’s voice. It would also explain why it knew me.”  
>
> “I did not tell the Department that my mother was employed at Bellwether House before I was born.”  
>
> “She left service abruptly in 581. Her diary says only: _I opened the blue door and found my son waiting._”  
>
> “I was born in 582.”

---

## APPENDIX: ON THE ETHICS OF ENTRY

Students frequently ask whether it is permissible to enter a persistent lacuna.

The answer is no.

Entry constitutes an attempt to complete the absence-event. In the majority of cases, completion is impossible: the expected person has died, the expected moment has passed, the expected world has altered beyond recovery.

Where completion is attempted, the lacuna does not disappear. It widens, incorporating the entrant as an additional unfulfilled expectation.

For this reason, all field personnel must maintain the **Threshold Rule**:

> **Never cross a boundary whose principal function has been waiting for you.**

> **Final marginal note, written in a different ink:**  
> “This rule is incorrectly phrased. The room was not waiting for me.”  
>
> “It was waiting for the version of me who never left.”  
>
> “At 23:40, every nullometer in the world began measuring the same absence because there is only one absence-event large enough to be everywhere.”  
>
> “The reading is not environmental.”  
>
> “It is biographical.”  
>
> “We are not studying what failed to happen.”  
>
> “We are studying the world that failed to contain us.”

dream_response:
### 1. Fragment of the Wetward Code, tablet 4

> **On Water Taken in Error**
>
> No person may claim rain before it has stopped moving.
>
> Water in the race belongs first to the race, second to the catchcloths, third to those who have set vessels beneath an agreed drip.
>
> A cup carried from the race during a red sounding is theft from all sleepers.
>
> A child may drink without measure.
>
> A sick person may drink without measure, provided two witnesses have heard the cough.
>
> The dead may not be washed in drinking water except in the season of first thunder.
>
> Whoever diverts a drip shall maintain the diversion until the next sounding, even if it wets their bedding, spoils their stores, or brings spiders.
>
> —copied by Nera-of-the-Ninth-Bay from the old clay, in the Year of the Bent Pipe

---

### 2. Recipe scratched onto the back of a detergent carton

## Roof-Mush and Pepper-Skins  
*For six, or three after a cold rain.*

Take:

- two fistfuls gray lace from the north wall;
- one measure of clean black water;
- half a packet of red powder, if the packet has not gone bitter;
- three beetle nests, emptied;
- the soft part of a fallen fruit, if the upper people have sent one;
- salt from the white crust, but only from above the yellow line.

Boil in a capped tin until the lace forgets it was lace.

Mash with a spoon-handle. Add pepper-skins last, so that the children can believe it is meat.

If there is no fruit, tell them the old story about the fruit river. This improves the taste but does not fill anyone.

—Moss Aunt Pev

---

### 3. Children’s game instructions, written in grease pencil on a flattened milk bottle

## PLAY “DON’T WAKE THE GRATE”

One child is the Grate.

The Grate lies with eyes shut and arms wide.

Everyone else is Drips.

Drips must cross the room without touching the loud places.

If a Drip makes a sound, the Grate wakes and points toward the White Mouth.

The pointed Drip must stand in the White Mouth until someone gives them a secret.

Good secrets:

- where the dry biscuit tin is;
- who has a spare wick;
- what the upper people are saying through the metal;
- who kissed whom behind the battery shelves.

Bad secrets:

- real hiding places;
- water counts;
- anything Mother said not to say;
- the names of the dead.

Last Drip uncaught becomes Grate.

**Rule for little ones:** Nobody is sent to the White Mouth during actual thunder.

---

### 4. Repair manual page, bound in thread made from plastic shopping bags

## Care of the Listening Wire  
*For municipal use. Do not lend to private rooms.*

The Listening Wire runs from the High Vent to the old lift-shaft mouth and tells us, by trembling, what the upper people are doing.

A slow trembling means carts.

A fast trembling means many feet.

No trembling at morning means they have closed the building or the wire is broken.

A hard three-beat knock, repeated, means machine work. Send someone to inspect the nearest hatch. Do not send Old Sena; she is brave but too large for the crawl.

If the wire goes slack:

1. Shut all singing.
2. Move the sleeping children away from the wall.
3. Check the copper hook at Bay Seven.
4. If the hook is gone, do not touch the exposed end. The upper people sometimes put fire into their wires.
5. Send notice to the Council of Drips.

**Important:** The wire does not predict rain. Anyone saying it predicts rain is thinking of the old wire, before the ceiling fall.

—Jor Bellmaker, apprentice repairer

---

### 5. Prayer copied into the margin of a medical booklet

> Keep the ceiling where it is.
>
> Let the black water run clear.
>
> Let the upper people remain hurried.
>
> Let them throw away soft fruit and unbroken packets.
>
> Let their machines speak loudly before they move.
>
> Let the grates rust slowly.
>
> Let no child be curious at the wrong opening.
>
> Let the Third Mouth stay open.
>
> And if it cannot stay open, let us know before it closes.
>
> —The Morning Request, said facing downstream

---

### 6. Municipal notice, printed on waterproof label stock

## NOTICE TO ALL RESIDENTS OF THE LOWER WARDS

By count of the Council, and with the agreement of the Waterkeepers, the following measures take effect at the next blue rain:

1. No lamp may burn after second sleep, except at the clinic, the hatch watch, and births.
2. All cloth shall be checked for mildew before trade.
3. The western crawl is closed to children, visitors, scavengers without rope, and anyone who has recently been above.
4. The annual naming feast is postponed until the Third Mouth speaks.
5. No one is to repeat the rumor that the upper people are “leaving forever.” Their carts continue. Their pipes continue. Their voices continue. Rumor is not evidence.
6. A reward of one dry battery and two meals is offered for verified information concerning the disappearance of the Third Mouth flow.

Signed under seal,  
**Dema Vale, Mouthwarden**  
Witnessed by **Ili, Keeper of Measures**  
Year 38 Since the Sheltering

---

### 7. A joke sheet passed between work crews

## WHY DID THE DRIP CROSS THE RACE?

To get to the dry side.

## WHY DID THE DRIP COME BACK?

Because the dry side was wet.

## WHAT DID THE UPPER MAN SAY WHEN HE DROPPED HIS KEYS?

Nothing. He said many things later, but none of them helped.

## WHAT IS THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN A COUNCILOR AND A RAT?

A rat can find food without holding a meeting.

## WHAT IS THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN A COUNCILOR AND A SPIDER?

A spider repairs its own web.

## WHAT IS THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN A COUNCILOR AND THE THIRD MOUTH?

The Third Mouth used to provide.

—anonymous, because Cowards Live Long

---

### 8. Scientific diagram on the reverse of a school attendance register

## THE GREAT PIPE, AS MEASURED BY STRING AND ECHO

```
                 [UPPER STREET]
      carts / feet / rain / shouting
                      |
                 iron circles
                      |
              ----- grate -----
                      |
              long fall of water
                      |
   old pipe ========(SPLIT)======== new pipe
                   /        \
          North Run          Third Mouth
          (cold, clean)      (warm, food-water)
                |                 |
          sleeping bays       fungus shelves
                |                 |
              White Mouth ---- old lift shaft
```

Notes:

- The old pipe slopes toward the river, except when it does not.
- The North Run smells of stone and soap after first rain.
- The Third Mouth smells of bread, fruit, grease, and sometimes blood.
- In the first years, the Third Mouth ran every seventh day.
- It now runs without pattern.
- When it stops entirely, the gray lace turns yellow within four sleeps.
- The upper people do not know which mouth is ours.
- We must not make them know.

Question for pupils: Why is the Third Mouth warmer than the North Run?

Correct answer: because it comes from places where upper people keep their food.

Incorrect answer: because the Pipe has a heart.

—Master Orr, School of Bay Five

---

### 9. Personal letter, folded inside a waterproof glove fingertip

My dear Lio,

Your aunt says you are asking whether you may come down to the White Mouth with the repair crew.

No.

You are eleven, and you have the shoulders of a coat hook. You cannot crawl safely through the broken bend, and you still hold your breath when frightened. That is dangerous. The White Mouth is not a story place. It is where the old elevator shaft opens into our pipe, and if you slip past the railing there is no bottom anyone has found.

I know your grandfather went there. Your grandfather also ate a lightbulb once because someone told him it was an egg.

You say you want to see daylight. I understand. I saw it once when I was little, during the Year of the Fire Door. It was not gold. It was white and enormous, and it made every dust-speck visible. I cried until my eyes swelled shut.

There are beautiful things here too. Last night the water made a silver ceiling in Bay Two. Your brother slept through it, so you may tease him.

Do your sums. Feed the lace. Do not trade your socks for cards.

I will be home after the next sounding.

Love,  
Mother

---

### 10. Extract from the trade ledger of the East Catchcloth Market

| Received | Given | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1 intact orange, upper-fall | 4 candle stubs | bruised but sweet |
| 3 packets salted crackers | 1 copper button, 2 beetle jars | packets damp at corners |
| 1 blue medicine bottle, empty | 6 measures clean water | useful glass, no stopper |
| 2 batteries, small | 1 blanket patch | both weak; test before birth use |
| 1 newspaper strip | 1 meal | headline says “Drainage Improvements Begin Monday” |
| 1 newspaper strip | 3 meals | same headline, different date; buyer claims it is newer |
| 1 yellow glove, unbroken | 2 wicks | child size, excellent for collecting fungus spores |
| 1 white mask | refused | smell of sharp cleaning liquid; burn outside |
| 1 tin peaches, unopened | 12 meals | opened at council table; pits saved for tools |

Margin note:

> The newspaper strips have caused panic twice. In future, no upper-writing is to be read aloud before the Council confirms whether “Monday” has already happened.

—Tovin, market counter

---

### 11. Funeral speech, transcribed from memory by a young clerk

We return Sana of Bay Seven to the water that fed her, though not to the fast water, because she feared the fast water.

She was born during the Long Dry, when people boiled condensation from the ceiling and called it soup.

She repaired thirty-two catchcloth frames.

She delivered sixteen children, including two who now complain that the world has no room for them.

She taught us that mold is not dirt until it changes color, that a rat bite is less dangerous than a proud person, and that one must never trust a pipe which has been quiet too long.

When the ceiling fell in the southern crawl, Sana was the first to enter and the last to leave. This is why her left hand never closed again.

Some say she was afraid of the Third Mouth. She was. Courage is not the absence of fear. Courage is keeping the food shelves full while fear is dripping down your neck.

Go gently, Sana. May the race carry you past the white foam, past the iron teeth, and into the wide dark where nothing leaks.

---

### 12. Emergency bulletin found sealed inside a jar of instant coffee

## TO EVERY BAY, EVERY CRAWL, EVERY CATCHCLOTH

The Third Mouth has not flowed for nineteen sleeps.

This is longer than any stoppage recorded after the Bent Pipe.

The upper voices are louder than usual. The Listening Wire reports repeated hard three-beat knocks from above the western wall. The ground has shaken twice. At second sleep, dust fell from the ceiling near the old market.

No one is to enter the western crawl.

No one is to climb toward the food-water source.

No one is to remove the red cloth from the Council hatch.

Waterkeepers will begin rationing at dawn-sounding. Fungus shelves are to be harvested only under supervision. Beetle nests are breeding stock and may not be cooked except by order of the clinic.

There are reports that the upper people have placed new pipes beneath the market building. There are reports that they are sealing old pipes. There are reports that they are only repairing the road.

All three reports may be true.

Remember: we survived the Long Dry. We survived the Flood of Grease. We survived the Year the lights stayed on.

But survival is not a promise made by the Pipe.

It is a promise we make to one another.

—Dema Vale, Mouthwarden  
—Council seal omitted, as the seal was lost during evacuation of Bay Three

---

## Editor’s reconstruction

The artifacts appear to come from a community of perhaps sixty to one hundred people living inside an abandoned or bypassed section of a municipal stormwater system beneath a commercial building. The repeated references to carts, food-water, fruit, packaging, and “upper people” suggest that the settlement lies below or beside a supermarket, warehouse, or market district.

The inhabitants are probably human descendants of people who entered the drainage network during some past emergency, construction period, or sheltering event. “Year 38 Since the Sheltering” may date the founding of the community, though whether that refers to thirty-eight years or thirty-eight seasonal cycles is uncertain.

Their economy depends on runoff contaminated—usefully, from their perspective—with food scraps from above. They cultivate fungus, breed beetles, collect discarded goods, and carefully manage clean water. Their unusual laws and religion arise from the danger of flooding, contamination, cave-ins, discovery, and scarcity.

The principal crisis is the disappearance of flow from the “Third Mouth,” apparently a warm drainage branch carrying nutrients from the building above. Construction or drainage renovation may have rerouted or sealed it. Without that flow, their food system will fail.

The apparent contradictions are mostly geographic: some sources call the main pipe a route “toward the river,” while others describe water traveling unpredictably or “upward.” This is likely due to backflow during storms, multiple pipe elevations, and differing local notions of direction. “Daylight” is described as both a sacred beauty and a terrifying threat because few inhabitants have seen it directly.

The most troubling detail is the final bulletin’s missing council seal. The observer found it pressed into the inner lid of the coffee jar that contained the papers—freshly impressed in wax, on the inside, where no one sealing the jar from outside could have placed it.
